posts.summary: A brief post recommending adblockers to eliminate distracting website advertising. The author notes they've been free from ads for 2-3 years thanks to browser extensions, and encourages readers to adopt the same approach. Two specific adblocker tools are recommended along with a comparison resource.
key_points: Adblockers eliminate the distraction of website advertising
The author has used adblockers for 2-3 years without issues
Two recommended tools: Adblock Plus and AdBlock
money_quotes: I was a bit confused at first because I didn't have that problem the last 2-3 years I think.
You will look confused as I when you hear about advertising on web pages the next time because there isn't anymore.
posts.key_insight: Adblockers are a simple solution that makes web advertising a non-issue you'll eventually forget exists.

posts.summary: This brief technical post highlights a subtle but important difference between Oracle and Microsoft SQL Server: the ORDER BY clause behaves differently due to database collation settings. The author demonstrates that identical text data sorted in Oracle versus SQL Server produces different results unless you explicitly specify a compatible collation. By using SQL_Latin1_General_CP850_BIN2 collation in SQL Server, the ordering can be made to match Oracle's behavior.
key_points: ORDER BY behavior differs between Oracle and Microsoft SQL Server due to collation settings
The same text data will sort differently between the two databases by default
Using SQL_Latin1_General_CP850_BIN2 collation in SQL Server aligns ordering with Oracle
money_quotes: Like you would think ORDER BY is the same in Oracle as in the Microsoft environment, it isn't.
It depends on the Collation of the database.
posts.key_insight: Database collation settings cause ORDER BY to behave differently between Oracle and SQL Server, a subtle gotcha when migrating between platforms.

posts.summary: This brief post recommends Focus@Will, a music streaming service designed to enhance concentration while working or studying. The author shares that the service uses neuroscience-based music channels that aim to engage the brain's limbic system without causing distraction. The post includes marketing claims about the science behind the approach, citing partnerships with neuroscientists. Simon personally endorses it based on his experience using it during his bachelor's degree work.
key_points: Focus@Will offers music channels designed by neuroscientists to improve concentration
Mainstream music is argued to be counterproductive for focus because it's designed to engage emotionally and intellectually
The service claims to increase focus biomarkers by 12-15% and extend work session times significantly
The author found personal success using it during academic project work
money_quotes: Mainstream music lowers comprehension and creates distraction because it is designed to connect with you intellectually and emotionally.
It was unbelievable how focused and concentrated I worked on my project.
posts.key_insight: Purpose-designed background music may be more effective for concentration than mainstream music because it avoids the emotional and intellectual engagement that causes distraction.

posts.summary: A brief product recommendation for Bose QuietComfort noise-canceling headphones. The author endorses these headphones for anyone who travels frequently or works in shared office spaces with multiple people. While acknowledging the high price, he argues the active noise cancellation technology makes them worth the investment for blocking out ambient sounds like trains, flights, and conversations.
key_points: Active noise-canceling headphones are essential for frequent travelers and open office workers
Bose QuietComfort series effectively blocks monotonous ambient sounds
The high price is justified by the quality and functionality
money_quotes: They are not cheap, but they are best!
If you are traveling a lot or working in a shared room with more than 5 people, you have to buy them, you won't regret it!!
posts.key_insight: Quality noise-canceling headphones are a worthwhile investment for knowledge workers dealing with distracting environments.

posts.summary: This technical post addresses the challenge of dynamically generating partitions for SSAS (SQL Server Analysis Services) Cubes, which lacks built-in easy partition management. The author presents a SQL-based solution that uses a linked server to execute XMLA statements from T-SQL, enabling automated monthly partition creation. The approach involves creating stored procedures that generate and execute XMLA commands to delete and recreate partitions based on date ranges. This allows for programmatic cube partition management without relying on SSIS packages.
key_points: SSAS lacks native easy partition generation, requiring either SSIS packages or custom SQL scripts
Creating a linked server enables executing XMLA statements through relational T-SQL
The solution uses stored procedures to dynamically generate monthly partitions with configurable date ranges
The script handles both initial 'before' partitions and iterative month-by-month partition creation
XMLA commands are constructed as XML strings and executed against the Analysis Services linked server
money_quotes: There is no easy way to generate partition for SSAS Cubes by default
The trick is, to create a linked server, that you can execute XMLA statements with relational T-SQL
posts.key_insight: A linked server bridging T-SQL and XMLA enables automated SSAS cube partition management that would otherwise require complex SSIS packages.

posts.summary: This technical guide documents the challenges of migrating Oracle database views to Microsoft SQL Server, focusing on function compatibility differences between the two platforms. The author provides a comprehensive reference table mapping Oracle functions to their SQL Server equivalents, highlighting cases where direct translations don't exist. Special attention is given to numeric conversion pitfalls, particularly how TO_NUMBER, CONVERT, and CAST handle decimal rounding differently. The post includes practical SQL examples demonstrating these differences and provides custom SQL Server functions that replicate Oracle behavior for TO_CHAR, TO_NUMBER, MOD, INSTR, and SUBSTR.
key_points: Oracle and SQL Server have different built-in functions, requiring careful translation during migration (e.g., TO_NUMBER becomes CAST or CONVERT, NVL becomes ISNULL)
Decimal rounding behavior differs significantly between Oracle's TO_NUMBER (rounds at 16th decimal) and SQL Server's CONVERT with float (rounds at 3rd precision) or numeric (converts to integer)
Some Oracle functions like TRUNC, GREATEST/LEAST, INITCAP, and LPAD/RPAD have no direct SQL Server equivalents
Custom user-defined functions can bridge the gap, replicating Oracle behavior in SQL Server for functions like INSTR and SUBSTR with their full parameter support
money_quotes: The first thing you figure out is, that there's not the same built in functions on both sides.
It sounds like a very easy translation, but you have to be careful, for instance when it comes to decimals.
The built-in TO_NUMBER() function rounds at the 16th decimal place and if you use CONVERT, the behavior is a bit different.
posts.key_insight: Database migrations between Oracle and SQL Server require careful attention to function behavior differences, especially around numeric precision and rounding, where seemingly equivalent functions can produce different results.

posts.summary: This post continues a series on migrating from Oracle to Microsoft SQL Server, focusing specifically on performance challenges with recursive queries. The author encountered significant issues translating Oracle's optimized SYS_CONNECT_BY_PATH function to SQL Server CTEs, especially in complex nested views. The solution involved using inline user-defined functions and temp tables to dramatically improve query response times from non-responsive to 1-5 seconds. The approach maintained transparency for end users and applications by wrapping functions in views.
key_points: Oracle's SYS_CONNECT_BY_PATH is highly optimized and has no direct SQL Server equivalent, causing major performance issues during migration
CTE views work for simple recursive queries but fail with complex nested scenarios
Inline user-defined functions with temp tables can solve recursive query performance issues
Storing complex queries in temp tables before recursive operations simplifies the optimizer's work
The solution maintains user transparency by wrapping functions in views
money_quotes: This function is well know by the Oracle optimizer which makes this function unbelievable fast and there is no similar function on SQL Server
I had good performance measures on an easy simple query but invinitive response with more complex
In my case it solve the performance issue from not responding to 1-5 second each view
I stored this complex query to a temp table and queried recursively on this temp-table. With this the complexity for the recursive query disappeared
posts.key_insight: When migrating Oracle recursive queries to SQL Server, inline user-defined functions with temp tables can overcome the performance limitations of CTEs for complex nested views.

posts.summary: This technical post concludes a three-part series on migrating views from Oracle to SQL Server, focusing specifically on testing methodologies. The author demonstrates how to verify both performance and data correctness after migration using SQL scripts. For performance testing, he recommends SELECT COUNT(*) queries with cache clearing, providing a script to auto-generate these statements. For content comparison, he explains using linked servers and EXCEPT queries to identify discrepancies between Oracle and SQL Server view outputs.
key_points: Performance testing requires clearing cache before each run and using COUNT(*) queries across all views
Database linked servers enable direct comparison between Oracle and SQL Server views
EXCEPT queries can identify content differences between source and target databases
Auto-generating comparison scripts saves time when testing multiple views
Both performance and content accuracy must be validated in database migrations
money_quotes: When I implemented all about 20 views from Oracle to SQL Server, I had to test the performance and not least important, the content of the views if their still contain the same.
Don't forget to clear the cache before each run.
If you have only a few comparison you easily create EXCEPT-SELECT-Query and compare your Oracle Views over the database link to your Microsoft Views.
posts.key_insight: Successful database view migrations require systematic validation of both performance metrics and data content accuracy using automated comparison scripts.

posts.summary: Simon reflects on a personal experience from his apprenticeship where he learned the value of sharing knowledge freely. Initially reluctant to share exam summaries he and a friend created, he eventually opened up and discovered that sharing led to receiving more input, better relationships, and valuable discussions. He connects this lesson to the rise of open source software and encourages readers to embrace a sharing mindset.
key_points: Initial reluctance to share hard work is natural but counterproductive
Sharing openly leads to receiving more valuable input in return
The act of sharing builds appreciation, relationships, and friendships
Discussions arising from sharing generate new ideas and insights
Open source's popularity validates the power of collaborative sharing
money_quotes: The more you share the more you get
After I took that step, I received a lot more input for our summaries. Everyone knew we were working on them, so everyone sent us helpful information and content for the document.
People will appreciate that you share something they can use without asking for something in return. AND, later on, they will remember you when they find something that might be of interest to you!
Maybe even more valuable than this, are the discussions that arise from sharing, which will inevitably lead to many new ideas.
It's no surprise that open source software or open source, in general, is more popular than ever before as it is all about sharing and learning from each other.
posts.key_insight: Freely sharing knowledge creates a virtuous cycle where generosity is reciprocated with more input, stronger relationships, and collaborative innovation.

posts.summary: Simon shares his personal toolkit for productivity and daily computer use, covering six categories of tools: time tracking (RescueTime), clipboard management (Ditto/Paste), password management (LastPass/KeePass), screen capturing (Snagit), blue light filtering (f.lux), and bookmark syncing (Xmarks). He emphasizes that these tools have genuinely improved his workflow and encourages readers to try them. The post reflects his practical, hands-on approach to optimizing work habits through technology. He notes this is part one of a series, with more tools to follow.
key_points: Time tracking tools like RescueTime provide honest feedback about productivity patterns and reveal where time actually goes
Clipboard managers are underrated utilities that become indispensable once you start using them for work
Password managers solve not just password problems but also the challenge of remembering which username was used where
Screen color temperature tools like f.lux can meaningfully improve sleep quality for late-night computer users
Visual communication tools like Snagit make explaining things remotely much more effective than text alone
money_quotes: The good thing about it is that this tool is not going to lie to you, it tells you the full truth.
And you probably think now 'I don't really use that kind app', but I can tell as soon you used it, you don't want to miss it anymore, give it a try.
So many websites, usernames, emails and passwords have to be remembered, it's not only about the passwords.
It's so much easier to understand if you add a speech bubble or a little text next to the thing which is important.
At 9PM, 10PM, or 3AM, you probably shouldn't be looking at the sun.
Although this sounds a bit over promised, it can improve your sleeping quality drastically.
posts.key_insight: Small utility tools that run quietly in the background—tracking time, managing clipboards, storing passwords—compound into significant productivity gains when used consistently.

posts.summary: Simon introduces a blog series on Data Warehouse Automation (DWA), arguing that while the fundamental DWH architecture hasn't changed much since IBM created it in 1986, the approach to building data warehouses must evolve. He contends that traditional manual approaches are too slow for modern business needs, and DWA tools represent the future of the discipline. The post serves as a table of contents linking to deeper explorations of why DWA isn't more popular, its benefits, and available tools.
key_points: The core data warehouse architecture has remained remarkably stable since IBM's 1986 design
Traditional manual DWH development is too slow for modern business requirements
DWA tools will revitalize data warehousing's reputation despite competition from data lakes and big data
Data warehouses aren't going away despite the hype around newer technologies
money_quotes: Very long time ago, 1986, 31 years ago to be precise, IBM in Europe created the very first architecture of a data warehouse. And it seems to be a masterpiece as it didn't change much since.
No matter how we want to improve the architecture, it has to be automated as much as possible.
Nowadays it became too slow for serving the business needs doing it the traditional way.
I strongly believe that DWA tools are the future and will boost the Data Warehousing reputation back to earlier years.
I don't think DWH will go away anytime soon.
posts.key_insight: Data warehouse automation is essential because traditional manual development can no longer keep pace with modern business demands, and DWA tools will restore the relevance of data warehousing in an era dominated by big data hype.

posts.summary: Simon Späti advocates for Data Warehouse Automation (DWA) tools, arguing they dramatically accelerate delivery while maintaining consistency and quality. He addresses common objections including fear of losing control, vendor lock-in, need for skilled personnel, licensing costs, and speed concerns. Drawing from his experience on a year-long project delivering 162 tables in three months, he demonstrates that DWA enables parallel development and faster iterations impossible with traditional methods. He concludes that as businesses demand faster insights and agile delivery, DWA adoption is inevitable despite emotional resistance from developers fearing replacement.
key_points: DWA tools allow customization through templates while producing thoroughly tested, optimized code that often outperforms manual implementations
Lock-in concerns can be mitigated by choosing tools that export to standard formats like SSIS packages or using open-source alternatives like ODE
The meta-model consistency benefit ensures all developers follow the same methodology for logging, technical fields, and lookups - something difficult to maintain manually
A real project delivered 162 tables including 15 facts and 43 dimensions in three months with DWA, which would have been impossible with traditional methods
DWA automates boring repetitive tasks while leaving complex modeling decisions and business analysis to humans
money_quotes: Every one needs to make data driven decision faster, why not use a generator which gives you answers in days instead of months..?
If you don't trust in your employees why then even hire them in the first place?
You can achieve with existing resources more in the same time and don't even have to hire new employees
Data Warehouse Automation tools are here to automate the boring tasks and leave the fun stuff for the developer
I don't like having an emotional discussion, emotions only blur the picture. In my opinion, it should always be based on facts
The business wants to see results faster and faster also because they get used to the way of working with data lakes where data can be added immediately

As a developer, I'm looking forward to learn new exciting tools, deliver significantly faster, with higher consistency and leave the boring tasks to the tool. I will go for the potential and not fight against it.
posts.key_insight: Data Warehouse Automation isn't about replacing developers but freeing them from repetitive implementation tasks to focus on the genuinely challenging work of data modeling and business requirements.

posts.summary: Simon Späti makes a case for Data Warehouse Automation (DWA) as the next logical step in an industry where automation has transformed every sector. He argues that in an era where data is the new gold and time is precious, manually building data warehouses creates unnecessary friction and errors. DWA enables teams to deliver KPIs within weeks rather than months, shifting focus from technical plumbing to actual data analysis. The core value proposition is eliminating repetitive tasks like creating staging tables, managing schema changes, and handling slowly changing dimensions—work that follows predictable patterns and begs for automation.
key_points: DWA shifts the focus from building and hunting data to actually examining and analyzing it
Repetitive DWA tasks (staging tables, schema changes, SCD handling, indexing) follow patterns that make them ideal automation candidates
Automated ETL provides consistency, proven code templates, and automatic documentation generation
Testing data warehouses manually is nearly impossible but can be automated with DWA tools
Version control enables easy rollback and change management that traditional approaches lack
money_quotes: In a society where time flys remarkably fast and data became the new gold, it's crucial to have proper analyses or even data predictive trends to navigate the company in the best direction.
It's not the time to do recurring tasks manually over again and producing additional errors.
DWA enables you to concentrate on examining the data not hunting and building them
Testing can be automated (which is almost impossible doing it in the traditional way)
We do the same thing over and over again in Data Warehousing.
It is actually hard to find a reason not to automate.
Don't waste more time and work smart at the speed of business with DWA!
posts.key_insight: Data warehouse development is fundamentally repetitive work that follows predictable patterns, making it an ideal candidate for automation that frees teams to focus on actual data analysis rather than technical plumbing.

posts.summary: This 2017 post surveys the data warehouse automation (DWA) tool landscape, comparing market presence using Google Trends data. Späti distinguishes between model-driven approaches (design first, then find data) and data-driven approaches (generate models from available data). He references external resources including Hans Michiels' comprehensive comparison guide and survey results. The post notes that most vendors lack transparent pricing, with BimlFlex being a notable exception. WhereScape and TimeXtender lead in search volume, though the author acknowledges search data conflates company and product names.
key_points: WhereScape and TimeXtender dominate DWA tool searches, though results are skewed by company name overlap
Model-driven DWA ensures business requirements are documented upfront; data-driven suits agile methodology better
Most DWA vendors hide pricing, requiring custom quotes - BimlFlex stands out for transparent pricing
Data-driven approach lets developers show real results quickly, aligning with agile practices
The DWA market includes tools ranging from open-source to expensive enterprise solutions with varying features
money_quotes: You quickly generate a model with the data which is available
The benefit is you only work with data that is actually there and the business has already a real-life model that represents the data
This suits very well with the agile methodology of Data Warehouse Automation tools
One of the only ones who provide transparent pricing is BimlFlex
Other vendors are not transparent and hand over prices on request
posts.key_insight: Data-driven warehouse automation aligns well with agile methodology because it lets teams work with actual data and show real results to stakeholders quickly.

posts.summary: Simon advocates for Microsoft OneNote as his go-to tool for organizing all aspects of life, from work projects to travel planning. He argues that OneNote's hierarchical structure of notebooks, sections, and pages allows notes to grow organically without losing organization. The post uses a two-month Asia trip as a practical example of how OneNote handles complex, evolving projects. He emphasizes that the tool's real power comes from long-term use, becoming a single repository for all essential information across work, personal life, and education.
key_points: OneNote's nested structure (notebooks, section groups, sections, pages) allows organic growth without losing organization
The 'write first, structure later' approach accommodates projects that evolve unpredictably
Full-text search including text in images makes finding information fast
Shared notebooks dramatically reduce coordination meetings and communication overhead
Cross-platform sync and offline access make it a true 'single source of truth'
money_quotes: Maybe the biggest feature itself is to organisation and structure inside OneNote
The real power comes with time, the more and the longer you use it, the more it gets to your one and only place to go for
This is really the beauty of OneNote, while most times you have no clue how the structure will look like in the end
Write first, structure later
You're having all essential information any kind of types in ONE single place
Shared every note together which reduced the amount of communication and coordination meetings to an absolute minimum
posts.key_insight: The best note-taking system is one that lets you write first and restructure later, growing organically as your projects evolve.

posts.summary: This 2017 post clarifies the distinctions between data warehouses and data lakes, arguing against the prevailing notion that data lakes will replace data warehouses. Späti explains that data warehouses serve structured, slowly-changing analytical needs with ETL processes, while data lakes handle unstructured, fast-changing big data with ELT approaches. He advocates for maintaining both environments in modern organizations, using data lakes for exploration and data warehouses for structured, accessible reporting. The post also introduces Data Vault methodology as a bridge between the two paradigms and promotes Data Warehouse Automation tools as the future of DWH development.
key_points: Data warehouses and data lakes serve fundamentally different purposes: DWH for cleansed, structured analytics; data lakes for raw, unstructured exploration
ETL belongs to data warehouses (transform then load), while ELT belongs to data lakes (load then transform)
Big data extends and enriches data warehouses but cannot replace them—it's the traditional way of building DWH that's dead, not the concept itself
Data Vault methodology offers a flexible approach that combines benefits of both paradigms for adapting to changing business environments
Organizations should import valuable patterns discovered in data lakes back into data warehouses to make insights accessible to operational users
money_quotes: If you think of a DWH as a store of bottled water – cleansed and packaged and structured for easy consumption – the data lake is a large body of water in a more natural state.
Big data can extend and enrich a Data Warehouse, but cannot replace it.
It is not Data Warehouses that are dead, but the traditional way of designing and building them.
More data is always better but can also lead to more chaotic data stores where nobody knows what's in there.
The Data Warehouse is designed for slowly changing data; Data Lakes are designed for quickly changing data.
Your ETL tool was probably outdated before you were ready to deploy it.
posts.key_insight: Data warehouses and data lakes complement rather than compete—organizations need both, with data lakes for exploration and data warehouses for making insights accessible and structured for everyday business users.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that data engineering is evolving from traditional data warehousing, driven by the growing need for real-time data, scalable pipelines, and programmatic approaches over drag-and-drop ETL tools. He traces his own journey from BI engineer to data engineering, explaining how the role combines software engineering skills with BI expertise and big data capabilities. The post details how ETL processes, data modeling practices, and even organizational education around data are all transforming. Späti concludes that while data warehouses will always be needed for structured business data, the methods for building them are shifting toward engineering-centric approaches, making data engineers the natural evolution of BI engineers.
key_points: Data engineering combines software engineering, BI engineering, and big data skills - it's the evolution of traditional BI roles as data complexity grows
Python has become the 'Swiss Army Knife' for data engineering, used for data wrangling, ETL, API interactions, and workflow automation with tools like Apache Airflow
Traditional drag-and-drop ETL tools (Informatica, SSIS, DataStage) are being replaced by code-driven, programmatic platforms because 'code is the best abstraction there is for software'
Data modeling is shifting away from strict normalization, surrogate keys, and complex SCDs toward more flexible, collaborative approaches with dynamic schemas
Not every company needs data engineers - the role is most relevant for data-driven products or organizations dealing with high volume, variety, and velocity of data
money_quotes: If English is the language of business, SQL is the language of data and Python the language of engineering.
Code is the best abstraction there is for software rather than using drag and drop tools.
The transformation logic is of a higher need and shouldn't be locked away exclusively for BI developers.
Python is here to stay and it's becoming the Swiss Army Knife of programming languages.
Data engineers at larger companies tend to be more focused on 'enterprise' skills like ETL, BI, and data warehousing, whereas data engineers at smaller companies focus more on core technologies.

In order to get high quality and frequently updated data sets, it is important to distinguish between data pipelines that are done and cleaned by data engineers and all the others that are mostly exploratory.
To be more collaborative and work on same objects it is a necessary trade-off to loosen it up.
posts.key_insight: Data engineering represents the natural evolution of BI engineering as organizations shift from batch-oriented, tool-driven ETL to code-first, real-time data pipelines that require software engineering skills alongside traditional data warehouse knowledge.

posts.summary: Simon Späti shares his favorite productivity tools across several categories: instant translation (Google Translate's camera feature, Skype Translator), grammar correction (Grammarly), email management (Google Inbox with its bundling and snooze features), focus music (Focus@Will, Brain.fm, Noisli), noise-cancelling headphones (Bose QuietComfort), quick notes (Google Keep), and Chrome extensions (Momentum, OneTab). The post emphasizes how these tools collectively help manage communication, reduce distractions, and enable deep work. He particularly highlights the science behind focus music, citing studies showing how specially designed audio can enhance concentration without the distraction of regular music.
key_points: Google Translate's camera feature provides real-time translation of physical text, invaluable for living or traveling in foreign countries
Google Inbox's AI-powered bundling automatically organizes emails by trip, finances, and other categories, reducing email management overhead
Focus music apps like Brain.fm are designed by neuroscientists to enhance concentration without the emotional and intellectual engagement of regular music
Noise-cancelling headphones combined with focus music create an optimal environment for deep work
OneTab Chrome extension can save up to 95% of browser memory by converting open tabs into a stored list
money_quotes: It is crucial to get into a state of deep work if you want to get as much done and in the best quality.
Music is designed to connect with you intellectually and emotionally which lowers comprehension and creates a distraction.
Sometimes I get even a little headache as my brain is in such a deep state of concentration for an extended period.
If you travel or live in a foreign country, it can save your life!
Of course, Google will read your email content for that purpose, but they do that already so why not making use of it.
When your tabs are in the OneTab list, you will save up to 95% of memory.
posts.key_insight: Achieving deep work requires intentionally engineering your environment with the right tools—from scientifically-designed focus music to noise-cancelling headphones to email management systems that minimize distraction.

posts.summary: Simon Späti shares his experience transitioning from traditional corporate work to a location-independent lifestyle, documenting his journey from Copenhagen to Bali, Southeast Asia, and across Europe. He argues that working remotely while traveling enables deep focus and natural productivity breaks, creating an ideal work-life integration. The post outlines the mindset and skills needed for this lifestyle, emphasizing creativity, continuous learning, and starting small. He reflects on lessons learned including the importance of cross-cultural connections, stoic philosophy, and maintaining a home base while embracing mobility.
key_points: Remote work while traveling enables deep focus sessions of 2-3 hours with natural breaks during sightseeing that help when stuck on problems
The location-independent lifestyle requires specific values: creativity, loving your work, starting small, celebrating wins, and continuous reading
Stepping out of comfort zones by moving to unfamiliar environments forces rapid personal and professional growth
Having a home base is compatible with the nomadic lifestyle - you don't need to travel constantly
Cross-cultural exposure reveals our fundamental human similarities despite different backgrounds
money_quotes: While having a delicious coffee, I can get things done for 2-3 hours.
Value the time, spend it right, you can't buy it back!
Maybe most important, getting to know so many different cultures and realising that we are all the same, just with a different background.
You don't have to travel all the time. It's okay to have a base somewhere in the world, where you can enjoy old friends, family and loved once and prepare for the next trip.
That was the first step for me to get out of my comfort zone and force myself to learn a bunch of new things.
Start small, but start (now)
posts.key_insight: The location-independent lifestyle isn't about constant travel but about intentionally designing work around deep focus sessions while remaining open to growth through new environments and cultures.

posts.summary: Simon Späti examines why traditional OLAP cubes, particularly Microsoft Analysis Services, are becoming obsolete and explores modern alternatives. He identifies key limitations including lack of Azure cloud support, data size constraints, and the complexity of MDX query language. The post categorizes replacements into four groups: pure OLAP technologies (Druid, ClickHouse), cloud data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery), data virtualization tools (Dremio), and serviced cloud analytics (Looker, Sisense). He emphasizes that the choice depends on specific requirements around response time, data volume, and architecture goals, while also highlighting the critical importance of orchestrators and cluster-computing frameworks to complete the modern data architecture.
key_points: Microsoft Analysis Services cubes face fundamental limitations: no Azure cloud support for multidimensional models, data size constraints, and the difficult MDX query language
Four categories of OLAP replacements exist: pure OLAP technologies, cloud DWHs, data virtualization, and serviced cloud analytics - each with different tradeoffs
Cloud data warehouses can eliminate the cube layer entirely, saving development and maintenance time at the cost of query latency
Orchestrators like Airflow are 'one of the most critical tasks that gets forgotten most of the time' in modern data architectures
Data Lakes serve different purposes than OLAP - good for data scientists exploring data without sub-second response requirements
money_quotes: These cubes are a compelling and fast way to aggregate and pre-calculate all of your corporate-related data and makes it available to your operational business users.
One reason is that nowadays are countless persons wish to make use of data, that's why you might want to make it available to all of them.
The idea is to speed up your DWH and skip the layer of cubes. This way you save much time in development, processing and maintaining of cubes.
Without a clear sense of data lineage, accountability issues can arise, and operational metadata can be lost.
The building blocks of DAGs are data pipelines or following processes where the output from one process becomes the input for the next.
This is one of the most critical tasks that gets forgotten most of the time.
posts.key_insight: Traditional OLAP cubes are hitting their limits in the cloud and big data era, and organizations must choose from four distinct replacement strategies based on their specific requirements for query speed, data volume, and architectural complexity.

posts.summary: Simon Späti explores building a complete open-source data warehouse architecture using Apache Druid for storage, Apache Superset for visualization, and Apache Airflow for orchestration. He explains why Druid's column-oriented, distributed design excels at OLAP workloads despite SQL join limitations. The post advocates for moving transformation logic into code-based orchestrators like Airflow rather than traditional ETL tools. He concludes that while these open-source tools work well together, enterprise users may still want to invest in commercial visualization tools since that's what end users actually see.
key_points: Druid provides sub-second OLAP queries and real-time streaming ingestion with a scalable cluster architecture of Middle-Manager, Historical, and Broker nodes
Apache Airflow represents a paradigm shift from traditional ETL tools by keeping transformation logic in code (Python DAGs) rather than encapsulated in proprietary tools
The combination of Druid, Airflow, and Superset creates a viable open-source alternative to commercial data warehouse stacks
Druid's main limitation is poor support for SQL joins and advanced SQL capabilities
Enterprise adoption of open-source orchestration naturally follows when companies start using open-source data technologies
money_quotes: Instead of encapsulating your critical transformation logic somewhere in a tool, you place it where it belongs to inside the Orchestrator.
In more modern architectures, these tools aren't enough anymore. Moreover, code and data transformation logic are much more valuable to other data-savvy people in the company.

My experience so far is that Druid is bloody fast and a perfect fit for OLAP cube replacements in a traditional way, but still needs a more relaxed startup to install clusters, ingest data, view logs etc.
Apache Airflow and its features as an orchestrator are something which has not happened much yet in traditional Business Intelligence environments.
I believe this change comes very naturally when you start using open-source and more new technologies.
As an enterprise company, you might want to spend some money in that category because that is what the users can see at the end of the day.
posts.key_insight: Modern data architectures benefit from separating concerns across specialized open-source tools—columnar storage for speed, code-based orchestration for maintainability, and lightweight visualization for accessibility—rather than relying on monolithic proprietary ETL platforms.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that email is an outdated and inefficient communication method for modern workplaces, causing constant interruptions that destroy productivity and deep work. Studies show workers switch tasks every three minutes, largely due to email, with each interruption requiring 20 minutes to regain focus. The post advocates for replacing email with structured project management tools like Basecamp, Slack, or GitLab that centralize communication, reduce noise, and align with how millennials prefer to work. Späti emphasizes that changing communication isn't just about tools—it requires a cultural shift toward valuing focused, productive work over constant availability.
key_points: Email functions as an open to-do list that others control, causing workers to switch tasks 36 times per hour and destroying deep work
Every interruption requires approximately 20 minutes to regain focus, making constant email checking extremely costly to productivity
Modern project management tools centralize discussions, tasks, and files in one place, eliminating the need to synchronize across scattered email threads
Workplace communication changes require cultural shifts, not just technical tools—especially as millennials become the dominant workforce
Open offices and unstructured meetings compound the email problem; offices should function like libraries where quiet, focused work is the norm
money_quotes: Email can be seen as an open to-do-list for everyone else to put a task on top of it.
It's ineffective because it's based on old fashion expectations. We approach email like a letter, where every email will be answered immediately the moment the letter arrives.
Without email, our informants focused longer on their tasks, multitasked less, and had lower stress.
Your brain is wired to listen to it, even though you don't want to, you will always find yourself listen to it.
Think of meetings as you have an expense on it.
It's not only a technical adjustment but more a change in culture.
Start with a small intimated group and only expand when the idea, the story gets more matured.
posts.key_insight: Email's fundamental flaw is that it puts control of your attention in everyone else's hands, making deep work nearly impossible without deliberate structural and cultural changes to workplace communication.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that traditional BI suffers from speed, transparency, and accessibility problems that data engineering approaches can solve. He presents twelve techniques including data lakes, Delta Lake for transactional processing, notebooks for democratizing access, Python/SQL pipelines, open-source tooling, incremental loading, and declarative programming. The post advocates for moving away from rigid data warehouse paradigms toward more flexible, transparent architectures that empower all data-savvy users. For organizations lacking resources, managed platforms like Ascend.io or Palantir Foundry offer these capabilities out-of-the-box.
key_points: Traditional BI is plagued by slow integration, lack of transparency, dependency on BI engineers, and difficulty handling unstructured data or real-time requirements
Data lakes with Delta Lake provide speed, schema flexibility, ACID transactions, and time-travel capabilities that solve many DWH limitations
Notebooks democratize data access but need governance to prevent code duplication and silo recreation
Incremental loading and idempotency enable near real-time analytics while eliminating lambda architecture complexity
Declarative pipelines (defining 'what' not 'how') represent the evolution from cron to modern orchestration tools
money_quotes: BI should produce a simple overview of your business, boost efficiency, and automate repetitive tasks across your organisation.
The BI department makes it more complicated than it requires to be.
Business keys are already understood by everyone, to some degree unique, holding the same value for the life of the data set, can be tracked and compared therefor all the way back.
The downside of notebooks is that your code will be duplicated and scattered around the place. A little bit like everyone having their own Excels.
Python is the tooling language of data engineers these days.
Be open to trying out along the way as you are not locked-in, you can always replace or adapt later on.
With the incremental approach, you switch from batch to event-driven. Your updates and inserts are independent, and you get autonomous batches.
Declarative programming is a paradigm that expresses the logic of a computation without describing its control flow.
posts.key_insight: Modern data engineering techniques—data lakes, transactional processing, notebooks, and declarative pipelines—can transform opaque, slow BI systems into transparent, accessible platforms that empower all data-savvy users, not just BI specialists.

posts.summary: Simon Späti presents a comprehensive hands-on data engineering project using a real estate web scraping use case to demonstrate a modern open-source data stack. The project combines web scraping with Beautiful Soup, S3 storage via MinIO, Delta Lake for ACID transactions and schema evolution, Apache Druid for OLAP, Superset for visualization, and Dagster for orchestration—all running on Kubernetes. The post serves as both a practical tutorial and an architectural guide, showing how these tools fit together in the Databricks Lakehouse paradigm. He emphasizes cloud-agnostic design and provides all code in public GitHub repositories for others to learn from.
key_points: Web scraping can treat any website as a database, and checking for open APIs via browser dev tools can simplify data extraction significantly
Delta Lake provides database-like features (ACID transactions, schema evolution, time travel, MERGE operations) on top of distributed files in S3
MinIO provides an S3-compatible gateway that enables cloud-agnostic object storage, allowing easy switching between AWS, Azure, and GCP
Dagster's high-level abstraction separates business logic in solids from technical code in resources, with elegant mode switching between dev/test/production environments
Kubernetes has become the de-facto standard for cloud-native data applications, enabling infrastructure as data via YAML rather than infrastructure as code
money_quotes: Starting with web scraping gives you the power to treat every website as a database.
To get database alike features on top of your S3 files, you simply need to create a Delta Lake table.
Data pipelines start simple and straight-forward, but often they end up vastly heterogeneous with various APIs, Spark, cloud data warehouse, and multi-cloud-providers.
Dagster lets you focus on building your data pipelines. It is made for data-flows and with it to pass data between the solids.
You have an elegant way of separating business logic in solids and technical code within resources.
Kubernetes has become the de-facto standard for your cloud-native apps to (auto-) scale-out and to deploy your open-source zoo fast, cloud-provider-independent.
Testing data is very hard and nothing compared to software testing as data is and even tools and framework is dynamic and can change every output of your transformation.
posts.key_insight: A modern data engineering project can be built entirely with open-source tools—from scraping to visualization—running cloud-agnostic on Kubernetes, with Delta Lake providing the database features needed on distributed files and Dagster orchestrating the heterogeneous components.

posts.summary: Simon Späti documents his migration from WordPress to Hugo, a static site generator. After years of struggling with WordPress plugin management, template incompatibilities, and the friction of converting markdown content to WordPress format, he found Hugo offered a simpler, git-based workflow. The migration was straightforward using an export plugin, and while page speed improvements were modest, the real value was in treating content as code—editable locally, version-controlled, and deployable with simple scripts. He describes the move as a 'homecoming' to the simplicity of his early web development days.
key_points: WordPress plugin management became unsustainable—constant updates, compatibility issues with Gutenberg, and production-only editing created ongoing maintenance burden
Static site generators like Hugo enable a code-like workflow: markdown files, git versioning, local preview, and simple deployment
Migration from WordPress to Hugo was surprisingly easy using existing export tools that converted posts to markdown with images intact
Page speed improved but wasn't the main benefit—the real win was workflow simplicity and having content accessible as files rather than locked in a database
The experience rekindled his enthusiasm for writing by removing friction from the publishing process
money_quotes: I always fancied something that works well with git and treats the text as code.

The real power for me, at least, is having the text right in front of me as markdown same as code, easily pushable to git. Compared to before, where it was hidden away in a MySQL database with no real access.
It surprised me how much fun it can be to write and generate new content again! I guess only for that fact I will create more content in the future than I did lately.
I feel like going back to where it started in 2005, where everything is small and simple in one (git) repo.
The best part is that security, scale and performance are improved radically with static site generation.
posts.key_insight: Treating blog content as code—version-controlled markdown files with local preview—removes enough friction that it actually makes writing fun again.

posts.summary: Simon Späti explains his migration from OneNote to Obsidian after 10+ years of note-taking. His core requirement was data ownership through plain-text Markdown files stored locally, rejecting proprietary formats that lock users in. After evaluating Notion, Roam, Joplin, and plain Markdown, he chose Obsidian because it's free, works as a wrapper around local folders and Markdown files, and offers features like Graph View. He provides Python scripts to help others export their OneNote notebooks to Markdown, demonstrating the technical process of escaping proprietary note-taking ecosystems.
key_points: Data ownership is paramount - notes should be plain-text Markdown files you control, not trapped in proprietary formats
Obsidian is free, works offline, and is essentially a beautiful wrapper around folders and Markdown files with one .obsidian config folder
Collaboration features are not essential for personal knowledge management - export to HTML/PDF when sharing is needed
Migrating from OneNote to Markdown is technically challenging but achievable with Python scripts and the Azure API
The flexibility of plain text means you can use any editor (vim, VS Code) or automate changes with scripts later
money_quotes: My goal was to own my notes as it's my data and my knowledge.
I didn't want to migrate from one proprietary format into the next one to lock me in yet another time.
Obsidian is just a wrapper around your folders and Markdown files, including one single folder called .obsidian in your root. That's it!
As everything is now plain text, I can search, replace or write a small Python script to fix these things later on.
In case you want to open all notes in one go, use open Obsidian.
posts.key_insight: Own your notes by storing them as plain-text Markdown files locally - this gives you freedom from vendor lock-in and enables any future tooling or automation you might need.

posts.summary: Simon argues that GraphQL can serve as the foundation for a unified 'Analytics API' that abstracts away heterogeneous data stores and provides a single interface for all data consumers. He outlines five key components: a GraphQL gateway/query engine, a metrics store (Headless BI), a data catalog, an orchestration layer, and a SQL connector. The post addresses how different stakeholders—ML engineers, BI users, power users, internal pipelines, and external customers—all need data access but through different interfaces. By centralizing metrics definitions and using GraphQL's built-in type validation, self-documentation, and schema decoupling, teams can avoid duplicating business logic across tools while maintaining flexibility to swap underlying data stores.
key_points: GraphQL solves for data engineering what it solved for microservices: unifying multiple backend systems into a single, intelligent endpoint instead of many dumb ones
The Metrics Store (Headless BI) centralizes calculated measures and dimensions that are typically locked inside proprietary BI tools like Tableau or Power BI
API schema decoupling from database schemas creates a semantic layer that allows swapping backends without breaking downstream consumers
Different stakeholders (ML engineers, BI users, power users, pipelines, customers) have fundamentally different data access needs that a unified API must serve
Built-in type validation, self-documentation through introspection, and standardized specification make GraphQL hard to avoid for complex data architectures
money_quotes: Essentially, GraphQL is SQL on steroids for APIs.

Instead of defining the metrics such as distinctUsers in various places, we store it once and apply it to all systems. No need to worry if you got the latest version or if anyone changed the calculation.
See it as certain business logic, critical code or metadata implemented once centrally following the DRY principles.
Despite how far we came with all data tools and frameworks, people are still running around and matching up correct numbers, and fighting who's once are correct.
Instead of having multiple dumb endpoints, you have a single intelligent endpoint that can take in complex queries.
The Jinja SQL logic is hard to re-use across different database engines and gets messy quickly if you start nesting these queries.
It is Google Search but for metadata with a handy interface for your metrics store.
posts.key_insight: The next evolution in data engineering is building a unified Analytics API with GraphQL that centralizes metrics definitions, decouples API schemas from databases, and serves all stakeholders through one intelligent endpoint instead of fragmenting business logic across tools.

posts.summary: Simon Späti presents a comprehensive personal knowledge management (PKM) system that combines multiple methodologies including the Second Brain, Zettelkasten, and Getting Things Done. The post argues that a well-designed PKM system reduces stress and enables a 'deeper life' by externalizing thoughts and tasks into a trusted external system. Starting from minimalism and vim's distraction-free environment, Simon built a workflow incorporating smart note-taking, linked thoughts, and systematic task management. The deeper insight is that PKM is ultimately about life itself—what we value, how we learn, and who we want to become.
key_points: Writing is not the outcome of thinking but the medium in which thought occurs—smart note-taking involves connecting thoughts rather than brainstorming from blank pages
The brain cannot distinguish between 'finished' and 'written down', so externalizing tasks and thoughts provides genuine mental relief
The PARA structure (Projects, Areas, Resources, Archive) with no more than four folder levels matches how brains naturally organize information
GTD's power comes from separating task capture from task review, and making tasks actionable rather than vague
A PKM system only works if your brain trusts it—the challenge is keeping the system updated so you don't feel compelled to remember things yourself
money_quotes: Being in a terminal with no buttons and no fancy UI, my brain focused on the essentials.
Writing is not the outcome of thinking; it is the medium in which thought occurs.
Your brain will not rest until a task is finished. As we can't complete all tasks on the spot, it helps that it does not distinguish between finished or written down.
The challenge is to keep your system up-to-date so that the brain trusts it.
Minimalism with less stuff gives you clarity and more freedom.
Instead of brainstorming, you connect thoughts and naturally generate new ideas.
Having spare money on the side is the best thing you can do for a deeper life.
Time is the currency of life, and wasting time is wasting money.
posts.key_insight: A personal knowledge management system is not merely about organizing information—it's a framework for living deliberately, reducing cognitive load, and freeing mental space for what truly matters.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that data orchestration is shifting from task-centric pipeline thinking to data product-centric approaches. Traditional orchestrators like Airflow focus on modeling task dependencies, but modern tools like Dagster and Prefect enable declarative, data-aware pipelines where data assets are first-class citizens defined ahead of runtime. This shift aligns with Data Mesh principles by making pipelines understandable to business users through a Data Product Graph that shows upstream dependencies and business logic. The post compares orchestration approaches, explains key abstractions (jobs, tasks, resources, triggers), and positions this evolution as essential for managing increasingly complex data environments.
key_points: Data orchestration is evolving from task-dependency modeling to data product-centric approaches where assets are declared ahead of runtime
Declarative pipelines offer advantages over imperative ones: faster developer cycles, better stability, compile-time optimization, and pre-runtime understanding
Modern orchestrators provide key abstractions (resources, tasks, triggers, data products) that enable code reuse and battle-tested implementations across complex cloud environments
The Data Product Graph represents the convergence of these trends - showing business users upstream dependencies and logic rather than technical pipeline details
Dagster excels at higher-level data engineering problems with data integrity focus, while Prefect offers fast, dynamic orchestration with simpler scaling
money_quotes: A Data Orchestrator models dependencies between different tasks in heterogeneous environments end-to-end.
In the end, all data must come from somewhere and go somewhere. Modern data orchestrators are the layer that interconnects all those tools, data, practitioners, and stakeholders.
A data asset is typically a database table, a machine learning model, or a report – a persistent object that captures some understanding of the world.
Similar to how DevOps changed how software gets deployed with Kubernetes and descriptive YAML, the exact same should happen with data pipelines.
Declarative approaches appeal because they make systems easier to debug and automate.
Abstractions let you use data pipelines as a microservice on steroids.
We have seen the same story over and over again. Products start small, in time, add adjacent verticals and functionality to their offerings, and become a platform.
posts.key_insight: The future of data orchestration lies in treating data products as first-class declarative objects that business users can understand, rather than focusing on the technical details of task dependencies and pipeline execution.

posts.summary: This comprehensive guide explains the differences between data lakes, data lakehouses, and data warehouses, focusing on how open-source table formats (Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg, Apache Hudi) transform simple cloud storage into powerful analytical platforms. The post details the three-layer architecture: storage layer (S3, Azure Blob, GCS), file formats (Parquet, Avro, ORC), and table formats that provide database-like features. Späti argues that data lake table formats are essential because they add ACID transactions, schema evolution, time travel, and unified batch/streaming capabilities to distributed files. The piece arrives at a particularly heated market moment, with Databricks open-sourcing Delta Lake 2.0 and Snowflake announcing Iceberg integration.
key_points: Data lake table formats (Delta Lake, Iceberg, Hudi) solve the fundamental problem of managing hundreds of distributed files by bundling them into queryable tables with database features
The lakehouse architecture combines data lake flexibility and cost-efficiency with data warehouse features, eliminating the need for Lambda Architecture's separate batch and streaming pipelines
All three major table formats share core features: ACID transactions, schema evolution, time travel, partitioning, compaction, and unified batch/streaming support
Delta Lake has the most GitHub stars and maturity after 2.0 release, Hudi has been open-source longest with most features, and Iceberg supports the most file format integrations
Without proper data governance, a data lake quickly becomes a data swamp with orphaned files and no structure
money_quotes: A data lake also removes the need to go through a proprietary format that traditional BI tools had done to transform the data.
With a data lake, data becomes increasingly available, and early adopters discovered that they could extract insight through new applications built to serve the business.
Imagine inserting into hundreds of files at once. They are the key and usually relay on one of the above, open-source data lake file formats that optimize columnar storage and are highly compressed.
These data lake table formats are the engine of the data lake file format.

Unified batch and streaming mean the Lambda Architecture is obsolete. No need to differentiate your data architecture in batch and streaming—they end both in the same tables with less complexity and more speed.

A lakehouse is more open (open-formats) and more difficult as more DIY and patching different tools together, supporting more ML/DS/AI use cases whereas a data warehouse is more closed (mostly closed-source), build for BI, fully managed, and more expensive to scale.
There is a big run for data lake table formats; every big vendor is either having one themself or searching for the perfect open-source one.
posts.key_insight: Data lake table formats are the critical missing layer that transforms cheap, chaotic cloud storage into governed, queryable analytical platforms with database-like capabilities.

posts.summary: The semantic layer is a translation layer between data and business users that defines metrics once and syncs them across multiple tools (BI, notebooks, data apps). Unlike transformation layers that pre-calculate data, semantic layers operate at query time, offering flexibility without storing multiple table versions. The concept dates back to SAP BusinessObjects in 1991 but has resurged with modern tools like Cube.js, MetricFlow, and dbt Metrics. While it promises centralized business logic, reduced duplication, and better lineage, it adds operational complexity and maintenance costs. The semantic layer intersects with OLAP cubes, data virtualization, data mesh, and data catalogs, each serving related but distinct purposes.
key_points: Semantic layers define metrics once in YAML/code and sync across BI tools, notebooks, and data apps, avoiding siloed metric definitions
Unlike transformation layers (dbt) that pre-calculate at ETL time, semantic layers transform at query time, enabling ad-hoc flexibility without storing multiple table versions
The concept originated with SAP BusinessObjects in 1991, evolved through OLAP cubes and LookML, and resurged in 2021 with tools like Cube.js, MetricFlow, and dbt Metrics
Key benefits include centralized definitions, DRY principles across tools, data lineage, and performance optimization through caching
Major challenges include integration complexity, learning curve, maintenance costs, and potential for faulty SQL generation across different dialects
money_quotes: Instead of re-creating siloed metrics in each app, we want to define them once, open in a version-controlled way and sync them into each visualization tool.
Because everyone has different definitions of 'active' users or 'paying' customers, the semantic layer allows you to define these discrepancies once company-wide.
A semantic layer transforms/joins data at query time, whereas the transformation layer does during the transform (T) step of ETL/ELT, meaning it's pre-calculated.

Suppose we would push all metric logic into the transformation layer, with dbt, for example. In that case, we'd lose this agility and store multiple versions of each table with different granularities.
With the fast-rising complexity of data and its ecosystem, you also need better abstractions!
The cost of maintaining and creating such a layer is high.
It's still easier to maintain copies of data sets and re-do the metrics repetitively inside a BI dashboard.
posts.key_insight: The semantic layer's resurgence stems from the explosion of data tools creating metric silos, and while it promises centralized, version-controlled business logic, organizations must weigh this against the operational complexity of adding another critical layer to their stack.

posts.summary: Simon Späti examines whether Rust could become a significant language for data engineering, concluding that while Rust won't replace Python, it's increasingly powering the backend tooling that data engineers use. He highlights Rust's advantages—strong typing, compile-time error checking, memory safety, and performance—as particularly valuable for data engineering's many moving parts and unreliable data sources. The ecosystem is growing with projects like DataFusion, Polars, and Delta Lake Rust, but Python's massive ecosystem inertia means Rust will primarily serve as infrastructure beneath Python wrappers rather than as a primary language for most practitioners.
key_points: Rust's compile-time type checking and error handling prevents costly runtime failures—especially valuable when dealing with unpredictable incoming data
The Rust data ecosystem is maturing with tools like DataFusion, Polars, and Delta Lake Rust, though most will be accessed through Python wrappers
Python's dominance in data engineering is protected by decades of tooling and ecosystem inertia that won't substantially change in the next decade
Rust's role mirrors what Scala was supposed to be—backend tooling for fast, well-maintained core components with Python interfaces on top
Writing Rust libraries feels like building long-term infrastructure rather than application code
money_quotes: Will Rust kill Python for Data Engineers? If you only came here to know this, my answer is no. Betteridge's Law strikes again!
Was Python made for Data Engineering in the first place?
When you write any code, the goal is that the code doesn't break during the weekend or at night when you sleep.

Rust's developer experience goes much further than just offering a language specification and a compiler; many aspects of creating and maintaining production-quality software are treated as first-class citizens.
It's pretty hard to imagine that inertia changing substantially in the next decade.
What was once supposed to be Scala will now be Rust—a backend tooling language to do tasks that need fast and well-maintained code, including a Python wrapper on top.
Writing libraries in Rust feels more like writing long-term infrastructure than writing in higher-level languages such as Python, Java, or the JVM.
posts.key_insight: Rust won't replace Python for data engineers, but will increasingly power the core infrastructure underneath Python wrappers—much like what Scala was supposed to become.

posts.summary: This post demonstrates how to use Dagster's Python-based configuration to dynamically create Airbyte data integration pipelines, contrasting it with static YAML configurations via Octavia CLI. Simon builds a demo that scrapes GitHub repository links from an 'Awesome Data Engineering' list using Beautiful Soup, then uses Dagster to programmatically configure Airbyte sources, destinations, and connections to track stargazer trends. The key advantage is handling dynamic, event-driven scenarios where configurations depend on external factors that change over time. The post walks through the complete stack: Airbyte for data integration, dbt for transformations, Dagster for orchestration, and Metabase for visualization.
key_points: Dagster's Python-based Airbyte configuration enables dynamic, event-driven data integration that static YAML configs cannot handle
The approach supports multi-tenancy, evolving APIs (like Notion), and metadata-driven pipeline provisioning
Software-Defined Assets in Dagster treat destination tables as Data Products with full lineage and metadata visibility
The dagster-airbyte CLI provides check and apply commands similar to infrastructure-as-code tools like Terraform
Everything becomes versioned and testable, bringing traditional software engineering practices to data integration
money_quotes: These features open instrumental use cases for data integration as code.
What now if you are dependent on incoming metadata that can change? If the configs are not hard coded, you'd need a script on top to generate the configurations dynamically.
Imagine you need to provision Airbyte, have multi-tenancy requirements for teams or customers, or read from a dynamic API.
Based on these configs, you can automatically apply new sync based on the latest status. Everything is versioned, which leads to changes with confidence.
The Pythonic definition of all Airbyte components opened the pandora box for more event-based use cases orchestrated by Dagster.
Software-Defined Asset in Dagster treats each of our destination tables from Airbyte as a Data Product.
posts.key_insight: Python-based configuration of data integration tools like Airbyte enables dynamic, event-driven pipelines that can adapt to changing metadata and external inputs, bringing infrastructure-as-code principles to data engineering.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that the 'Open Data Stack' is a better framing than 'Modern Data Stack' because it emphasizes tools built on open source and open standards, which integrate better and reduce vendor lock-in. He presents four core tools as essential for any data-driven company: Airbyte for data integration, dbt for transformation, Metabase for analytics, and Dagster for orchestration. The post contrasts the old way of writing custom scripts for each data task with the modern approach of leveraging battle-tested open-source components. He emphasizes that this stack is modular and extensible, allowing teams to add specialized tools as needs grow.
key_points: The 'Open Data Stack' emphasizes open source and open standards over just being 'modern', enabling better tool integration and avoiding vendor lock-in
Four core tools form the foundation: Airbyte (integration), dbt (transformation), Metabase (visualization), and Dagster (orchestration)
The old approach of writing custom scripts for extraction, scheduling, and monitoring created unmaintainable, siloed solutions that broke without warning
dbt is called the 'king of SQL' because it forces software engineering best practices onto SQL transformations including testing, documentation, and version control
Dagster enforces functional data engineering principles—declarative, idempotent, type-checked functions that are testable in isolation
money_quotes: The Open Data Stack is a better term for the Modern Data Stack, but focusing on solutions built on open source and open standards covering the data engineering lifecycle.
With the open data stack, companies can reuse existing battle-tested solutions and build on top of them instead of having to reinvent the wheel.
Every data project starts with some SQL queries. The default should be to use dbt, which immediately forces you to use the best software engineering practices.
Write business logic with just a SQL select statement or a Python DataFrame that returns the dataset you need, and dbt takes care of materialization.
As data pipelines quickly grow in complexity and data teams grow in numbers, using methodologies that provide clarity isn't a luxury—it's a necessity.
The beauty of the data stack is that you can now add specific use cases with other tools and frameworks.
posts.key_insight: The shift from custom data scripts to an open, composable data stack isn't just about using newer tools—it's about adopting battle-tested, interoperable components that enforce engineering best practices and eliminate the endless reinvention of foundational data infrastructure.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that Vim is not just an editor but a powerful language for text manipulation that every programmer should learn. He explains the grammar behind Vim - verbs, subjects, objects, and motions - which allows users to combine a few memorized commands into hundreds of combinations. After years of hitting productivity limits with standard editors, he discovered that Vim's modal editing approach optimizes for editing rather than writing from scratch. While acknowledging the steep learning curve, he shares how Vim transformed not just his coding but his entire workflow, from writing to browsing, making him a better developer through deeper terminal fluency.
key_points: Vim's power comes from its grammar-like language (verbs, subjects, objects) that lets you combine commands rather than memorize thousands of shortcuts
Vim motions are universal and available in other editors, browsers, and applications - you can learn them anywhere
Vim is the only editor that optimizes for editing existing text rather than writing from a blank page, using modal editing (normal, insert, visual, command modes)
The learning curve is steep but pays off - Vim is the only editor where you get faster with time
Learning Vim naturally improves terminal and Linux skills, leading to broader developer competency
money_quotes: I've since learned that Vim is the only editor that you get faster using with time.
Everyone who types on a computer eight hours a day should learn the Vim language.
Vim is the only editor that optimizes editing text instead of writing from a blank page.
With a couple of verbs and objects, you can already know hundreds of combinations without memorizing each one individually.
It felt liberating, moving my cursor with the precision of a surgeon.
An underrated skill in general among programmers is using the terminal.
Vim changed not only my workflow but how I was able to edit at the speed of thought.
Working in Neovim is one of the highlights of my everyday work.
posts.key_insight: Vim's grammar-based approach to text editing - combining verbs, motions, and objects - transforms memorizing shortcuts into learning a composable language that makes you faster over time rather than hitting a productivity ceiling.

posts.summary: Simon Späti examines why mid-to-large enterprises struggle to adopt the Modern Data Stack (MDS), despite its open-source advantages. He traces the typical enterprise journey from custom scripts and legacy systems through the technology adoption lifecycle, noting that MDS tools are still in their 'early market' phase. The post contrasts US startup culture's rapid adoption with European enterprises' focus on cost, privacy, and regulatory compliance. While acknowledging the complexity and integration challenges of modular MDS tools, Späti argues that open-source solutions offer enterprises significant opportunities in flexibility, legal compliance, and competitive advantage—provided they have the technical capacity to manage them.
key_points: Enterprise data environments are inherently complex due to multiple teams, legacy systems, technical debt from departed employees, and the need to integrate ERP, CRM, and various departmental tools
The Modern Data Stack is still in its 'early market' phase—most tools are brand new and haven't crossed the chasm to mainstream enterprise adoption
European enterprises differ significantly from US startups in their adoption patterns due to stricter privacy regulations (GDPR), cost optimization focus, and historical vendor lock-in experiences
Open-source MDS provides a game-changing opportunity for smaller companies to outperform larger enterprises with data-driven strategies and modern technology

The tradeoff between modularity and complexity is real: integrating good tools doesn't guarantee a good stack, and enterprises must honestly assess whether they have capacity to own and manage the stack themselves
money_quotes: My colleagues and I have essentially re-implemented the MDS repeatedly at each company in one way or another.

Today you get the full data stack for free, open-sourced on GitHub. It's a game-changer and is one reason that small companies can outperform large enterprises regularly with strong data-driven strategies.
Working with data within a big organization is complicated and unpredictable. On the surface, it might not look that challenging.
Integrating good tools doesn't mean you'll get a good stack and expected results. It's still a complex thing.
Large enterprises typically ignore the trends until they are unavoidable.
Your final choices have long-term consequences, as you lock the company into that proprietary tool.
Ask yourself: 'Do we have the capacity and knowledge to own and manage the data stack ourselves?'
posts.key_insight: The Modern Data Stack offers enterprises a path to escape vendor lock-in and gain competitive advantage, but adoption requires honest assessment of organizational capacity—integrating good tools doesn't automatically produce a good stack.

posts.summary: Simon Späti shares his personal journey of leaving the conventional 'Default Path' of career progression to forge his own 'Pathless Path' - a concept from Paul Millerd about embracing uncertainty over conformity. He traces how his supportive childhood, early passion for computers, sabbaticals in Australia and Bali, and relocating to Copenhagen shaped his identity. Along the way, he discovered stoicism as a guiding philosophy, fell in love with English as a creative medium, and ultimately found his life partner. The post concludes with his return to Switzerland, starting a family, and embracing remote work and writing - demonstrating that everyone's pathless path looks different.
key_points: A supportive childhood with unconditional love provides the foundation for taking risks later in life
Sabbaticals and mini-retirements at various life stages (not just at 65) offer crucial clarity and self-discovery
Learning English opened access to a world of knowledge and ultimately led to a passion for writing
Stoicism serves as a practical philosophy for navigating uncertainty and following intuition
The 'Pathless Path' isn't about rejecting all defaults - it's about consciously choosing your path rather than following a script
money_quotes: I have learned that some of my best decision-making comes from following my gut, heart, and intuition - a place of inner knowing.
The Pathless Path is experimenting on your own to find a life you want to live vs 'getting ahead' on a script others follow.
In these 'stress' situations, you get to know yourself and deeply engage with the surroundings and people around you.
Being a minimalist, I make impactful decisions. Saying no to things I didn't value meant I could follow my passions.
Ultimately, it is anyone's path; This was my Pathless Path, and it was, in a sense, genuinely pathless—following curiosity and my instincts.
Try to be happy with less. Try minimalism. Then, happiness = reality - expectations.
Finding love is likely to happen when you are your best self. Not when stuck in a daily commute or working 9-5.
posts.key_insight: The most meaningful life decisions come from training your intuition through experience and reflection, then having the courage to follow it even when it diverges from conventional expectations.

posts.summary: Pandas 2.0 represents a major evolution in the Python data manipulation ecosystem by adopting Apache Arrow as its backend, replacing NumPy. This shift addresses long-standing issues with memory efficiency, speed, and interoperability that plagued earlier versions. Arrow's standardized in-memory format enables zero-copy data sharing between tools like Pandas, Polars, and DuckDB, dramatically reducing overhead in data pipelines. The article surveys the broader ecosystem of alternatives including Polars (Rust-based with query optimization), DuckDB (SQL interface), and Dask (parallelization), noting that Arrow's adoption as a common standard makes switching between tools nearly seamless.
key_points: Pandas 2.0's adoption of Apache Arrow backend solves critical limitations around missing values, string handling, speed, and interoperability that NumPy couldn't address
