Zero-copy data access is the killer feature of Arrow - it allows sharing data between programs without serialization overhead, making terabyte datasets as accessible as megabytes
Polars offers a cleaner API than Pandas with built-in query optimization, while DuckDB provides a SQL interface that can query Pandas DataFrames and Arrow datasets directly
The new Arrow-based string type is 3.5x more memory efficient, significantly expanding how much data can fit in RAM on a single laptop
Arrow's standardization means converting between Pandas, Polars, and other Arrow-compatible tools is nearly instantaneous with minimal overhead
money_quotes: Apache Arrow allows sharing data in an in-memory representation without persisting.
Zero-copy data access, mapping complex tables to memory to make accessing one terabyte of data on disk as fast and easy as one megabyte.
If you do not need to persist the data set at each step, that's a tremendous boost in performance and cost.
The bulk of the data, GBs or TBs, must not be serialized into another format. This allows data sharing easily, even with 'big data'.
Polars is the more accessible version of Spark and easier to understand than Pandas.
Whatever tool you use, once Pandas 2.0 is used, if you use Arrow's types, converting between Pandas, Polars, and others will be almost immediate.
posts.key_insight: Apache Arrow's emergence as the universal in-memory data format is reshaping the Python data ecosystem, making tool choice less consequential since zero-copy interoperability means data flows seamlessly between Pandas, Polars, DuckDB, and other Arrow-compatible systems.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that data modeling is the overlooked foundation of data engineering that deserves renewed attention. He traces the history from Codd's relational model through Kimball and Inmon's data warehousing approaches to modern techniques. The post explains why data modeling fell out of favor—rushed MVPs, small teams under pressure, lack of time—and why it's resurging as organizations realize that solid data architecture determines the success of data-driven culture. Späti introduces key concepts including normalization, metrics/KPIs, slowly changing dimensions, and ERDs as foundational knowledge for data engineers.
key_points: Data modeling creates a structured representation of organizational data that serves as a blueprint for business value, yet most projects initially ignore it due to time constraints and fast-paced development
The history of data modeling spans from 1960s hierarchical models through Codd's relational revolution, Kimball/Inmon's data warehousing approaches, to modern techniques like data vault and data mesh
Organizations often build 'quick and dirty' MVPs under pressure, then patch them for production instead of stepping back to build proper foundations
Data complexity grows exponentially without proper modeling—similar to Big-O notation, data copying can reach O(N²) if you don't manage how data flows through the organization
The resurgence of data modeling interest comes from organizations realizing that solid data architecture and tool choices directly impact data-driven culture success and costs
money_quotes: Most data projects initially ignore data modeling because of time constraints, fast-paced development, or sometimes lack of knowledge.

Instead of investing time into sketching out how the data should flow from its source, the organization's requirements, to how the end user wants to analyze its data, projects get built quickly and with a 'dirty' MVP.
Due to a lack of time and resources, these small teams get pressured to deliver a valuable product to the business immediately after showing a working MVP.
If you do not know about Big-O notation, you are probably not delivering on good performant code.
If we see data engineering through the lens of data modeling, I am optimistic that we have an angle to work against these forces and keep the complexity of storing data low.
People have realized that a solid data architecture and choice of tools matter a lot regarding the success of a data-driven culture and the cost of using data.
Instead of thinking in your small garden ahead, you zoom out and see the whole field.
posts.key_insight: Data modeling is experiencing a well-deserved resurgence because organizations have learned that skipping foundational data architecture in favor of quick MVPs leads to exponentially growing complexity and costs.

posts.summary: This second part of a data modeling series explores the approaches and techniques essential for structuring data effectively. The post contrasts top-down approaches (starting with conceptual models from business requirements) with bottom-up approaches (starting from existing physical data sources), recommending a combination of both. It surveys key modeling techniques including dimensional modeling (still highly relevant despite being from 1996), data vault modeling for handling big data and changing connections, anchor modeling for schema flexibility, and bitemporal modeling for historical data tracking. The author emphasizes that poor data modeling decisions manifest as problems in the 'last mile' of analytics, making early attention to modeling critical for project success.
key_points: Top-down and bottom-up approaches to data modeling serve different purposes: top-down works best with clear business requirements, bottom-up is beneficial for legacy systems or unclear requirements
Dimensional modeling remains highly relevant today because it forces data engineers to consider critical questions about granularity, entities, and metrics upfront
Data Vault modeling addresses modern challenges of big data and fast-changing data by combining 3NF and Star Schema methodologies into a more flexible structure
Common data modeling problems include embedded business rules causing cascade changes, incorrect entity identification leading to duplication, and lack of standardization preventing data sharing
Pain from poor data modeling decisions appears in the last mile of analytics, but the root causes originate in the first part of the data cycle
money_quotes: Data literacy is the ability to derive meaningful information from data, just as literacy, in general, is the ability to derive information from the written word—or, said differently, extrapolating the business value from the data given.
Is dimensional modeling still needed within data engineering compared to its popularity way back? The answer is a resounding yes—perhaps even more so than before.
By incorporating a robust dimensional model at the core of every data project, data engineers are compelled to consider critical questions related to granularity, entities, metrics, and more.
Data modeling is easy to neglect; assessing the consequences can take time and effort.
If you initially ignore poor data modeling and architecture decisions, you'll likely notice problems in the last mile, thinking they might be due to the tools or insights.

The benefit of a conceptual model in a larger enterprise organization of sufficient complexity gives you a great framework to order the logical and physical models around, decoupled from the source systems.
posts.key_insight: Dimensional modeling remains essential in modern data engineering because it forces teams to address fundamental questions about business value, granularity, and entities upfront—problems that will otherwise surface as mysterious failures in the analytics layer.

posts.summary: This final installment of Simon Späti's data modeling series examines how data architecture patterns influence modeling decisions, from the enduring staging-cleansing-core-mart layers (rebranded by Databricks as bronze-silver-gold) to specialized patterns like batch vs streaming, lakehouse vs warehouse, and semantic layers. The post surveys data modeling tools across commercial and open-source options, explores whether AI can automate data modeling (concluding it cannot replace human domain understanding), and introduces frameworks like ADAPT and BEAM for structured modeling approaches. Späti argues that despite tool proliferation and AI hype, the core challenge remains translating business semantics into data structures—work that requires deep domain knowledge and human judgment.
key_points: The foundational data warehouse architecture (staging→cleansing→core→mart) from 2016 remains highly relevant today, just rebranded as medallion architecture with bronze/silver/gold layers
Streaming is still mostly optional for analytics—hourly batching satisfies most business needs, and real-time adds significant complexity with limited recovery options
AI cannot automate data modeling because it requires understanding business semantics and domain knowledge, not just pattern recognition—the hard part is translating code to real-world meaning
The semantic layer represents a significant architectural choice, potentially replacing logical models by storing queries in declarative YAMLs executed at runtime rather than persisted physically
Start with simplicity in architecture decisions—you can always add complexity later, but premature optimization toward streaming, microservices, or data mesh often backfires
money_quotes: Isn't it amazing that something from 2016 is still so current? That's why data modeling is getting into vogue again, as it has never been entirely outdated.
Still, to this day, streaming is primarily optional. Suppose you tell the business team you can achieve near real-time with hourly batching; they will be happy.
As smart as ChatGPT might be, it would need to understand the semantics of how these IDs coalesce into something meaningful to automate any step of modeling or ETL.
If there is a place where you do not want to use automation, then it would be in data modeling and the overall data architecture.
A data engineer will always be needed to assess the outcome, understand and make sense of the data in front of him and understand the overall data flow of the organization.
Whatever you choose, start with simplicity. You can always add complexity later when the product and solution mature.
The essence of data modeling is simplicity, no matter how complex the underlying data might be.
posts.key_insight: Data modeling remains fundamentally about translating business semantics into data structures—a task requiring human domain expertise that AI cannot automate, despite advances in generative AI.

posts.summary: Simon Späti recounts his journey from traditional editors like Notepad++ and VS Code to adopting Vim and Markdown as the foundation of his entire workflow. He describes how learning Vim's modal editing philosophy transformed his efficiency, comparing it to surgical precision with text. The transition from WordPress to static site generation with Markdown files eliminated formatting battles and gave him a single source of truth. Combined with Obsidian for note-taking (with Vim keybindings enabled), he now writes everything from notes to a full book in plain text, publishing with simple scripts.
key_points: Editor shortcuts and productivity gains were lost whenever switching tools, but Vim motions work everywhere
Vim's modal editing philosophy (normal, insert, visual, command modes) enables surgical precision with text that traditional editors cannot match
Moving from WordPress to Markdown static sites eliminated the hassle of maintaining two versions and formatting battles
Obsidian with Vim keybindings combines the best of both worlds: note-taking optimized UI with Vim's editing superpowers
Plain text as the foundation allows editing everything with muscle memory and sharing content across blog, second brain, and book with single scripts
money_quotes: That's how a surgeon would work: fine-grained and filigree, but with words.
I can edit my second brain, and text I write everywhere with the speed of thought.
It felt powerful. I had one version of the truth. No more formatting battles, no more fear I'd lose a bolding text or a code formatting.
It was like learning a game, adding a new superpower (shortcut) every week.
The problem was that I lost my productivity whenever I switched to another editor, program, or app.
Whenever I switched editors to a newer, better one, I had to re-learn all shortcuts and mappings.
posts.key_insight: Adopting Vim motions and Markdown as universal tools creates a compound productivity advantage that transfers across all text editing contexts, from code to notes to long-form writing.

posts.summary: Simon Späti details his comprehensive Obsidian-based note-taking workflow, explaining how he evolved from scattered notes across multiple apps to a unified 'Second Brain' system. He emphasizes using plain text Markdown files for future-proofing, combining PARA and Zettelkasten methodologies for organization, and leveraging Vim motions for efficient navigation. The workflow includes templates for different note types, a tag-based system for note maturity levels, and public sharing via Quartz. His core argument is that this system provides clarity, peace of mind, and the ability to find knowledge intuitively through graph-based connections rather than rigid folder structures.
key_points: Plain text Markdown files ensure notes remain accessible forever, regardless of what happens to Obsidian as a tool
The combination of PARA (folder structure) and Zettelkasten (atomic notes with links) eliminates the cognitive overhead of deciding where to store notes
Vim motions enable mouse-free, effortless navigation and note creation that becomes second nature
Sharing notes publicly creates a feedback loop that accelerates learning and connects you with like-minded people
The graph-based approach allows searching by association (finding a place through remembering a person) rather than rigid categorization
money_quotes: It's not about which tool you use, as you can achieve the same with any other.
Even after Obsidian might die one day, as they are just simple text files with Markdown, they can be opened by any text editor in the past and future.
I do not need to spend a thought on where to store my note as one note can potentially belong to many different areas of my life.

The satisfaction I get from you guys giving me feedback, telling me that it was helpful, pointing out some alternatives or just making friends online, is something you can't replicate in the real world.
This happens more often lately that I do google less, but instead search my second brain as I have written it down as I googled it already more than once.
Never would I have thought, as an organized Swiss person, that I would leave the path of putting everything into folder structures to find easily.
Imagine if everyone would update their articles or notes instead of creating copies repeatedly; the internet would get a web of remarkable, highly valuable notes.
posts.key_insight: A well-designed personal knowledge management system should prioritize finding information through intuitive connections and associations rather than rigid folder hierarchies, using open formats that outlast any single tool.

posts.summary: DuckDB is an embedded analytical database that combines SQLite's simplicity with columnar-vectorized query execution for analytics workloads. The post categorizes DuckDB's enterprise value into five key use cases: interactive data apps, on-demand pipeline compute, lightweight SQL analytics, zero-copy SQL connector, and secure data handling. For enterprises, DuckDB offers significant benefits including reduced infrastructure costs, simpler deployment, and the ability to run complex analytics locally without network latency. The emerging 1.5-tier architecture, enabled by MotherDuck, allows the same DuckDB engine to run in both browser and cloud, fundamentally changing how analytical applications are built.
key_points: DuckDB fills the 'embedded + analytical' quadrant that SQLite fills for transactional workloads, offering vectorized query execution for fast columnar processing
Five enterprise categories: Interactive Data Apps, On-Demand Pipeline Compute, Lightweight SQL Analytics, Zero-Copy SQL Connector, and Secure Enterprise Data Handler
Enterprises can save significant costs by replacing cloud compute with local DuckDB for testing, development, and data exploration without data movement
The 1.5-tier architecture moves compute to the browser/edge, eliminating network roundtrips and enabling faster analytical experiences
DuckDB's embedded nature enhances security by keeping all compute within existing processes, simplifying compliance for sensitive data
money_quotes: Think of it as SQLite for analytics workloads but with a fast columnar-vectorized query execution engine.
Loading CSVs and precisely correcting data types is still not a solved problem in 2024.
Think of it as the Swiss army knife of data engineers.
Simplify. Replace Apache Spark with DuckDB where possible. Spark is a complex setup, even more so for tuning and debugging.
As DuckDB can be embedded into data operations, all compute is done within the existing process.
Developers can move the data closer to the application or user, making the analytical experience magnitudes faster as you save the roundtrips from the client to the server.
Not many other databases can handle such a broad range of use cases, so it's hard to explain DuckDB to someone new.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB's unique position as an embedded analytical database enables enterprises to simplify infrastructure, reduce costs, and achieve faster insights by bringing compute closer to data and users.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that the data stack is evolving toward declarative, code-first approaches where entire pipelines—from ingestion to visualization—can be defined in configuration files rather than imperative code. He traces declarative computing's history from Fortran to SQL to Kubernetes, showing how abstracting the 'how' empowers non-programmers while maintaining software engineering best practices. The post introduces a conceptual framework where a complete data stack could be recreated from a single function like `run_stack(serve(transform(ingest)))`, separating stateless configurations from stateful data assets. Späti acknowledges this unified declarative stack doesn't fully exist today but sees tools like Dagster, SQLMesh, and Rill as moving in this direction.
key_points: Declarative systems abstract implementation details, letting users specify 'what' they want rather than 'how' to achieve it—enabling non-programmers to build data infrastructure
YAML has become the lingua franca of declarative configuration because it's less verbose than XML/JSON and optimized for human readability
The ideal declarative data stack separates stateless configuration (reproducible) from stateful data assets (the actual data), enabling confident reproducibility
Tools like SQLMesh advance beyond dbt by actually parsing SQL semantically, enabling data-aware operations like automatic backfill detection
A 'Maslow's hierarchy of state' progresses from basic declarations to self-actualizing systems that can dynamically adapt to changes
money_quotes: Call it Infrastructure as Code, Visualization as Code, BI as Code, or anything as code. YAML engineering, or DSL—no matter what, it's the way the data stack evolved to empower non-programmers to create data pipelines and infrastructure without losing the benefits of SW engineering best practices through declarative methods.
A declarative data stack cleanly separates semantics from implementation, decoupling the business code from technical implementation, the how is rendered by the data stack engine.
As dbt doesn't know anything about its SQLs, it just runs them; it's limited to doing anything data-aware.

Also, if you are declarative, you can make the code work for a very long time. For example, the browser runs the code from Craigslist from 1995, and it's only possible to this day because HTML is declarative.
Think of Markdown. It is universally portable, and almost every application can render it with its various engines.
Once you have a declarative data stack, you essentially have your low-code or no-code platform, as you can build an API/UI on top of your configs.
posts.key_insight: A truly declarative data stack would allow the entire pipeline to be recreated from configuration files alone, treating infrastructure like source code while cleanly separating the unchanging logic from the ever-changing data.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that Generative Business Intelligence (GenBI) represents a paradigm shift in how business users interact with data, enabled by the declarative BI-as-Code approach where dashboards and metrics are defined in YAML rather than hidden in GUI configurations. The key insight is that declarative definitions provide the semantic foundation AI models need to understand business logic, relationships, and metrics. GenBI promises to overcome the traditional bottleneck of data teams by allowing natural language conversations to generate dashboards and metrics. Späti positions this as the next evolution of self-service BI, with tools like Rill Developer already implementing these capabilities through LLM-powered dashboard generation.
key_points: GenBI requires a declarative foundation - dashboards and metrics stored as code provide the semantic context AI models need to understand business logic
The metrics layer is critical infrastructure, serving as the semantic foundation that defines measures, data model relationships, and business definitions that AI can learn from
BI-as-Code enables a workflow where humans and AI collaborate through GitHub PRs, iterating on prompts until artifacts are production-ready
GenBI addresses the long-standing self-service BI challenge by enabling domain experts without SQL skills to create and iterate on dashboards through natural language
The approach scales from simple metric creation to complex data modeling, mapping conceptual dashboards through logical metrics layers to physical table joins
money_quotes: At its core, GenBI delivers an unreasonably effective human interface, where we iterate quickly, based on BI-as-Code.

Self-service BI has been around for a while, trying to enable business users to create dashboards and reports. But that transition has always been hard, and without SQL or even Python skills to wrangle and clean your data, it's hard. I think GenBI is the next attempt for Self-Service BI, which is very promising.

While AI can control visual interfaces, this approach is inefficient for complex business logic. The data model joins, metric aggregations and bus matrix require explicit declarations that AI can parse and understand; print-screened images are insufficient.
Code-first enables a smaller data team to do more. Think of the Ruby on Rails developer, who does end-to-end from changing the database theme to the front of the app.
If we can further automate this process, we will overcome one of the biggest problems of BI: the bottleneck of the data or BI teams to ship or publish data artifacts.
This is usually where technical data professionals care least, so a trained model that helps us would significantly boost productivity.
posts.key_insight: Declarative BI-as-Code is the prerequisite for GenBI because AI models need explicit, machine-readable definitions of business logic and metrics - not opaque GUI configurations - to generate meaningful dashboards and analytics.

posts.summary: This comprehensive guide catalogs 15+ companies using DuckDB in production across five key categories: zero-copy virtualized SQL, lightweight single-node compute, pipeline processing, embedded interactive apps, and enterprise security. The post demonstrates DuckDB's versatility through concrete case studies showing dramatic performance improvements, from Watershed's 10x faster analytics to FinQore's pipeline reduction from 8 hours to 8 minutes, and Okta's processing of 7.5 trillion security records. The author argues that DuckDB's simplicity, embedded nature, and performance make it uniquely positioned to simplify data architectures across diverse use cases, from browser-based analytics via WebAssembly to replacing expensive cloud data warehouses.
key_points: DuckDB excels in five distinct categories: zero-copy data virtualization, lightweight compute, pipeline processing, embedded interactive apps, and enterprise-grade security handling
Production implementations show dramatic performance gains: Watershed achieved 10x faster queries, FinQore reduced 8-hour pipelines to 8 minutes, and Hex saw 5-10x speedups in notebook execution
The zero-copy capability enables querying APIs, Hugging Face datasets, and data lakes directly without moving data, fundamentally changing data architecture patterns
Enterprise adoption is proven at scale: Okta processes 7.5 trillion records across 130 million files with thousands of concurrent DuckDB instances, handling data spikes from 1.5 TB to 50 TB daily
WebAssembly integration enables browser-based analytics, powering tools like Evidence, Rill, and government climate dashboards without server-side processing
money_quotes: Modern computers are powerful enough that many organizations can effectively run their analytics workloads on a single machine, making DuckDB an attractive option for those seeking a more straightforward, more maintainable data stack without sacrificing performance or features.

In six months, their defensive cyber operations team processed 7.5 trillion records across 130 million files using thousands of concurrent DuckDB instances, handling data spikes from 1.5 TB to 50 TB per day without infrastructure changes.
This approach dramatically reduced their data processing costs from $2,000/day with Snowflake while maintaining system robustness and security.
DuckDB's ability to efficiently handle Parquet files on S3 makes it particularly powerful for data lake architectures.

When writing queries in Python, DuckDB returns a 'relation' object (an abstract representation of the query) rather than immediately materializing the total result. This relation can be stored in a variable and used in subsequent queries, making SQL more composable.
Testing locally, 100 times a day, before going into production can save much money and speed up testing cycles.
The best way to understand is through hands-on experience. Import a CSV, wrangle some data, execute some queries, visualize a large local data set, or read distributed files from S3.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB has evolved from a lightweight embedded database into a production-ready analytics engine that dramatically simplifies data architectures while delivering order-of-magnitude performance improvements across diverse enterprise use cases.

posts.summary: This post introduces an analogy between the Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern from web development and modern data architecture's semantic layer. Späti argues that just as MVC separates presentation from business logic through a model layer, data teams need a similar separation between data storage and presentation through semantic layers. The post serves as an introduction to a longer article published on Cube.dev, positioning semantic layers as the data world's equivalent to Active Record in Ruby on Rails. The core thesis is that proven software architecture patterns can solve similar challenges in data engineering around metric definition and maintenance.
key_points: MVC's separation of concerns principle from the late 70s has direct parallels to modern data architecture challenges
Semantic layers serve as the 'model' equivalent in data, providing a declarative place to define metrics, measures, and business KPIs
Active Record patterns from Ruby on Rails map conceptually to how semantic layers work in data modeling
This architectural approach enables confident updates and versioning of business logic
The pattern addresses metric definition and maintenance challenges that plague data teams
money_quotes: Wouldn't it be convenient to separate the presentation from the storage through a data modeling layer, similar to the model layer?
A place where we can declaratively define our metrics, measures, and business KPIs so that we can update them confidently and version them.
Today, we have the beginning of such a separation of concerns with a Semantic Layer.
This architectural pattern can solve similar challenges in data engineering that MVC solved for web development.
posts.key_insight: The MVC pattern's separation of concerns, proven over decades in web development, provides a useful mental model for understanding why semantic layers matter in modern data architecture.

posts.summary: This is a teaser post for a full article published on Cube.dev about universal semantic layers in modern data architectures. The post introduces the challenge data teams face with multiple sources, BI tools, and the need for consistent metrics. It references a prior article exploring semantic layers through an MVC lens and promises deeper coverage of how semantic layers evolved, their components, benefits like unified access and security, and integration capabilities with SQL, GraphQL, and Excel APIs.
key_points: Semantic layers provide a single source of truth for business metrics while abstracting underlying complexity
Modern data teams struggle with multiple data sources, various BI tools, and self-service analytics demands
The semantic layer acts as both a controller for data access and a model for business metrics (MVC pattern)
Key benefits include unified data access, enhanced security, improved performance, and governance
money_quotes: Modern data teams face growing complexity: multiple data sources, various BI tools, and ever-increasing self-service analytics.
This is where a semantic layer shines: It provides a single source of truth for business metrics while abstracting away the underlying complexity.
Organizations must maintain consistency across their metrics while making data accessible to business users.
posts.key_insight: A universal semantic layer has become essential infrastructure for modern data teams, providing the single source of truth needed to maintain metric consistency while enabling broad accessibility across diverse tools and APIs.

posts.summary: This is a teaser post introducing the third part of a series on semantic layers, focusing on their integration with AI for natural language data querying. The post argues that semantic layers may be the best starting point for GenAI initiatives in data organizations. The full article, published on Cube.dev, covers the architecture of AI-powered semantic layers and real-world use cases. The post positions semantic layers as a solution to the growing complexity and data governance challenges organizations face.
key_points: Semantic layers may be the optimal starting point for GenAI adoption in data organizations
The integration of semantic layers with AI enables natural language data querying
Organizations struggle with data complexity and governance, making unified data access difficult
AI-powered semantic layers have practical applications in chatbots, dashboards, and data exploration
money_quotes: Data-driven decision-making is crucial for business success, but organizations face a growing challenge of complexity and data governance.
These challenges make it difficult to access data in a unified way.
In this final piece of the series, we examine the integration of a semantic layer with artificial intelligence and why it might be the best place to start with GenAI.
posts.key_insight: Semantic layers provide a structured foundation that makes them an ideal starting point for integrating GenAI capabilities into data infrastructure.

posts.summary: Simon Späti explores the architectural decisions required to build a declarative data stack engine (DDSE) that can generate entire data pipelines from configuration files. Drawing from his experience with data warehouse automation tools, he argues that separating business logic from technical implementation enables faster iteration, better testing, and maintainable data stacks. The article compares imperative versus template-driven approaches through code examples, examines whether existing orchestrators like Dagster could serve as the foundation, and surveys existing open-source tools in this space. He concludes that the path forward involves focusing on open standards and modular adapters rather than monolithic engines.
key_points: A declarative data stack separates business logic (what) from technical implementation (how), enabling config-driven pipeline generation that can be tested before production
The DDSE consists of three components: configuration (YAML defining sources, transforms, dashboards), engine (compiler that generates code), and declarative code output (Git-managed artifacts)
Data warehouse automation tools from 10 years ago achieved similar goals - prototype in days, iterate based on feedback, deploy instantly - and modern DDSEs should match this velocity
There's tension between parametric approaches (flexible but complex black boxes) and template-oriented approaches (forkable but you're on your own), suggesting a hybrid is needed
Modern orchestrators like Dagster already provide many DDSE features through annotations and could serve as one implementation path alongside Rust-based engines
money_quotes: Tools that lack these declarative code paths are difficult to automate, because code generation is almost impossible (imagine trying to auto-generate Tableau dashboards or Informatica pipelines).
Essentially, designing a compiler/makefile for the data stack declared from a single function or config file.
The business logic and technical logic are all mixed up. It's harder to read already with this simple example, but the more significant our project, the more unreadable such imperative code gets.
Everything a developer interacts with should be declarative, including infrastructure, runtime, type evolution, CDC logic, credentials, and schedules.
We developers shouldn't make imperative decisions about scope and execution; the engine should handle that.
When users fix an issue, they improve the engine's templates rather than modifying generated code, benefiting all future deployments.
Open standards form the foundation for openness and integration, decoupling the data stack, which is also crucial for creating a DDS engine.
posts.key_insight: A truly declarative data stack requires treating the entire pipeline as a compiler problem - configuration in, tested artifacts out - where fixes improve templates rather than generated code.

posts.summary: Simon Späti provides a comprehensive overview of the essential tools and foundational skills every data engineer needs, from operating system choices to Linux commands, Docker containerization, and development environments. He emphasizes that while the toolkit is extensive, engineers should focus on fundamentals and learn incrementally rather than trying to master everything at once. The article covers the practical building blocks: environment variables, virtual machines, CLI tools, simple orchestration with Makefiles and cron, IDEs, notebooks, and version control with git. He concludes that SQL and Python remain the core languages, with the reminder that sometimes the simplest Linux command can replace a complex toolchain.
key_points: Linux proficiency is essential for data engineers since most data platforms run on Linux-based servers, and fundamental CLI skills transfer across environments
Docker containers have become the standard for packaging data engineering work, enabling reproducible environments across local development, CI/CD, and production
Simple orchestration using Makefiles and cron jobs can be surprisingly powerful before reaching for complex tools like Airflow or Dagster
SQL remains the most important language for data engineers, followed closely by Python as the 'Swiss army knife' for building pipelines
The foundation of command-line tools and Unix philosophy (piping commands together) often provides simpler solutions than complex toolchains
money_quotes: To be proficient as a data engineer, you need to know various toolkits—from fundamental Linux commands to different virtual environments and optimizing efficiency as a data engineer.
Think of container packages on ships that transport goods; the breakthrough was the standardized container size that fits on every harbor could maneuver them.
You don't need to be an expert, but you shouldn't be afraid of command line tools and know some basic Linux commands.
The core responsibility of a data engineer is to orchestrate different jobs in the correct order and fully automate them.
In line with the Unix Philosophy, to make one tool do one thing as best as possible, you can combine 'pipe' different tools.
Instead of learning as many as possible, I suggest investing in a few used at your company and where you benefit most.
This foundation reminds us that sometimes the simplest solution is the most effective—a well-chosen Linux command can often replace a complex toolchain.
It's better to understand why than skip over it quickly.
posts.key_insight: Mastering foundational tools like Linux commands, Docker, and simple orchestration provides data engineers with more leverage than chasing the latest complex frameworks—sometimes a well-crafted shell pipeline is all you need.

posts.summary: Simon Späti traces the history and enduring relevance of pivot tables, from their origins in 1986 at Lotus through Excel's dominance to their modern renaissance in BI tools. He argues that pivot tables succeed because they align with human cognition through simplicity, intuitive constraints, and instant feedback - functioning as a 'REPL for tables.' The post examines how modern data tools like Rill, Cube, and DuckDB are integrating pivot table functionality, powered by fast OLAP backends. Späti predicts that pivot tables combined with AI (GenBI) will become the primary interface for non-technical users to explore data, especially as they become capable of querying data lakes directly.
key_points: Pivot tables are the 'Lingua Franca of data' - a common language understood by management, domain experts, and developers alike, serving as the first no-code data interface
The human factor explains pivot table success: well-defined constraints create simplicity, which builds trust and control, leading to standardization across tools
Modern BI tools are bringing pivot tables back because fast OLAP backends now enable 'Excel-like pivoting' at scale without Excel's limitations of being slow, local, and manual
The Lindy effect applies: having survived since 1989, pivot tables are 'Lindy Software' whose longevity predicts continued relevance
AI and GenBI will transform pivot tables into quick REPLs for AI to retrieve and interpret data, potentially running natively on data lakes without traditional databases
money_quotes: A pivot table is a REPL for tables. You can drill and slice; you get direct feedback, and it's fast and instant.
Allowing analysts from fifteen minutes of complicated data table and database functions to 'just seconds' of dragging fields into place.
If we can't understand and can't control it, we also trust it less. Trust is a human instinct that goes far. Once the trust in your data is lost, it's nearly impossible to get it back.
The enduring power of pivot tables is their robustness, simple usage, and fast, interactive response. It's the Lingua Franca of data if you are not fluent in the language of SQL or Python.
Pivot is the OG of a dashboard. Without a pivot table, how would you create a dashboard?
Is it worth spending hours perfecting a dashboard's aesthetics when you really need to understand how your company is performing?
The secret to their longevity might lie in their intuition interface through reasonable constraints and standardization around measures and dimensions.
posts.tone: reflective, historically grounded, optimistic about pivot tables' future while being practical about their enduring value
posts.key_insight: Pivot tables endure not despite their constraints but because of them - their rigid structure of rows, columns, filters, and values creates the intuitive simplicity that builds user trust and enables standardization across any tool.

posts.summary: Simon Späti explores how to achieve flow states and deep work in an age of digital distractions, drawing from personal experience as a self-employed author. He argues that tech companies manipulate us like lab animals through dopamine-driven engagement, while artists who focus on process over reward naturally enter flow states. The post offers practical strategies for creating focus-friendly environments, the importance of breaks and rest, and embracing slow productivity. He advocates for 'deep happiness' from mastery and meaningful work over 'shallow happiness' from quick dopamine hits, ultimately connecting flow to living a deeper, more fulfilling life.
key_points: Tech platforms manipulate behavior through dopamine rewards similar to how researchers condition lab animals, while artists escape this by finding reward in the creative process itself
Environment design is crucial for flow: dedicated browsers, offline tools like Obsidian, changing locations every 1.5-2.5 hours, and repetitive non-vocal music signal 'work time' to the brain

Breaks and rest are essential ingredients often overlooked—including vacations, good sleep (especially REM after 7-8 hours), and different types of rest (physical, mental, creative, emotional, social, spiritual, sensory)
Cal Newport's slow productivity principles (do fewer things, work at natural pace, obsess over quality) lead to a 'deeper life' that is more fulfilled and present
To unblock creativity, change the medium (writing to talking, digital to paper), add constraints, and practice doing nothing—unlimited freedom actually blocks creativity
money_quotes: Aren't they the same as the researcher, feeding us with dopamine to tell us to do what they want?
The drawing, the way to the end, is the reward, not the likes or selling the art.
Writing in the flow state, as I'm writing these words right now when I am merely in control, but the words flow out of my fingers. I guide them, but I do not write or choose the words.
There is no talking block, only writer's block.
Unlimited freedom is super-blocking, especially for creativity.
Writing an article or learning something hard, like coding or vim motions, is initially hard. But the reward is long-term. We get deep satisfaction from being in the flow.
I think of my creative process as gardening. Things inside us build up slowly, but great things will come out with constant nurturing.
When I don't have inspiration or I have a block, I do nothing. I live.
posts.key_insight: True productivity and creativity come not from optimizing for external rewards and constant stimulation, but from designing environments and habits that support flow states and embracing the slow, patient process of deep work.

posts.summary: This comprehensive tutorial introduces geospatial data processing using DuckDB, explaining why specialized spatial capabilities are necessary beyond traditional relational databases. The post covers fundamental GIS concepts including spatial data types, coordinate systems, and spatial indexing (R-trees), demonstrating how spatial queries can reduce computations from 100 billion to 10 million through efficient bounding box filtering. Using the Foursquare dataset as a practical example, Späti shows how to perform spatial analysis like finding chocolate store density across Swiss cities and identifying store clusters. The article positions DuckDB and MotherDuck as accessible alternatives to expensive traditional GIS tools, democratizing geospatial analytics for data engineers.
key_points: Spatial indexes using Minimum Bounding Rectangles can reduce geometric comparisons from 100 billion to ~10 million, making spatial operations dramatically faster than SQL joins
Geography is the second most used dimension after time/date in analytics, yet many data engineers haven't worked with spatial data because they lack customer-facing applications
DuckDB's spatial extension eliminates the need for complex setups like PostGIS + Python scripts, consolidating data preparation, integration, and analysis into a single tool
Traditional GIS tools like ArcGIS and QGIS created high barriers to entry that DuckDB now removes for common spatial analytics use cases
MotherDuck's cloud network significantly accelerates queries on remote datasets (1.8s vs 9s for the same Foursquare count query)
money_quotes: Besides the time/date dimension, geography is probably the second most used dimension.
With DuckDB, all your data preparation, integration, and analysis are consolidated into a single database. Spatial support is just an extension away.
Instead of 100 billion computations, spatial indexing may only need to look up 1M grid cells, each lookup comparing against nearby polygons (~10 instead of 100K).
GIS and maps are challenging, and there is a lot to cover.
Geospatial analysis powers countless daily applications—from delivery services to store locators—often invisibly enhancing our digital experiences.
With DuckDB, this analytical power becomes accessible to every data engineer, democratizing capabilities once reserved for GIS specialists.
Everything is hard. That's the thing with data visualization. You have to understand the data. You have to understand the context. You have to understand the technique.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB democratizes geospatial analytics by eliminating the infrastructure complexity and cost barriers that traditionally required expensive GIS software or complex PostGIS setups.

posts.summary: The post examines when organizations should move beyond Postgres for analytical workloads and explores the landscape of specialized analytical databases. It distinguishes between cloud data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery) optimized for scale but with higher latency, and real-time analytical databases (ClickHouse, Pinot, Druid) that co-locate compute and storage for sub-second queries. The author traces the evolution from early OLAP cubes through modern systems, noting that the industry has come 'full circle' in balancing performance with flexibility. The piece provides decision frameworks and migration guidance for choosing between these categories based on query patterns, cost constraints, and latency requirements.
key_points: Postgres is excellent for transactions but hits fundamental limits for analytics—extensions have diminishing returns since it's architecturally an OLTP database
Cloud data warehouses trade latency for scale through decoupled storage/compute, introducing seconds of 'incompressible latency' per query
Real-time analytical databases achieve sub-second performance by co-locating compute and storage, returning to principles that made early OLAP cubes successful
The evolution of analytical systems is cyclical rather than linear—modern systems address the same fundamental challenge of balancing performance with flexibility
Choosing requires weighing cost-per-TB-stored versus seconds-per-TB-scanned, with hybrid architectures often combining multiple approaches
money_quotes: Postgres is the Swiss Army knife of databases, and for many applications, it's more than sufficient.

Cloud data warehouses are built – like physical warehouses in the real world – to enable storage and retrieval at massive scales with efficiency. However, in order to achieve these efficiencies, they rely on an architecture that separates storage and compute, introducing seconds of incompressible latency for every query.
While the technical evolution of analytical systems appears to be a linear progression, it is cyclical, addressing the same fundamental challenge: balancing performance with flexibility.

Early OLAP systems achieved incredible speed through pre-aggregation but sacrificed flexibility. Modern systems return to OLAP principles with dynamic rather than static aggregation, enabled by hardware advances that early OLAP architects could only dream of.
The significant difference between these analytical databases is the trade-off between cost (dollars-per-TB-stored) and performance (seconds-per-TB-scanned).

At the end of the day, engineers need to choose an analytics database technology based on trade-offs across cost, performance, scale, and complexity—which ultimately only our business use cases can determine.
posts.key_insight: The analytical database landscape has come full circle—modern real-time databases return to OLAP principles of co-located compute and pre-aggregation, but with dynamic flexibility enabled by hardware advances, offering a compelling alternative to cloud data warehouses when sub-second latency matters more than infinite scale.

posts.summary: The article distinguishes between vector engines (like DuckDB) that use vectorized execution for analytical processing and vector databases (like Pinecone, Weaviate) designed specifically for AI embedding storage and similarity search. Späti argues strongly against building parallel data infrastructure for AI workloads, advocating instead for integrating vector capabilities into existing data engineering stacks. He examines how traditional databases are adding vector extensions and questions whether dedicated vector databases will remain necessary long-term. The piece concludes by positioning DuckDB as a practical bridge between data engineering and AI workflows, offering vector capabilities without requiring separate infrastructure.
key_points: Vector engines process data in chunks using CPU optimizations (SIMD, cache hierarchy) for analytical workloads, while vector databases store embeddings optimized for similarity search and RAG applications
Organizations should integrate vector capabilities into existing data platforms rather than building parallel AI infrastructure, following DRY principles
Traditional databases (PostgreSQL with pgvector, MySQL, SQLite) are rapidly adding vector extensions, blurring lines with dedicated vector databases
LangChain and similar frameworks often create redundant orchestration that duplicates existing ETL tools without meaningfully simplifying the core work of prompt engineering
DuckDB serves as a Swiss Army knife bridging data engineering and AI workflows with its vectorized execution and ability to store embeddings via Array/List types
money_quotes: The key is to not repeat ourselves.

What began as an abstraction layer for LLM applications has, for many teams, become a redundant orchestration tool that sounds and feels a lot like an ETL tool or orchestrator that teams already have in place.
As someone with two decades working with data, this lesson stands out above all others—avoid creating parallel systems for what could be extensions of existing ones.
Instead of multiple specialized tools for AI workloads, the sustainable path forward integrates vector operations directly into existing data platforms.
Vector operations should complement, not replace, your well-established data engineering practices.
Data engineers already excel at building reliable pipelines, transforming data, and ensuring consistency—these skills transfer directly to AI workflows when the right integrations are in place.
By prioritizing integration over isolation, data engineers can harness the power of vector technologies while building cohesive, maintainable data platforms that stand the test of time.
posts.key_insight: Rather than building parallel infrastructure for AI workloads, data engineers should integrate vector capabilities into their existing data platforms to avoid technical debt and leverage established expertise.

posts.summary: This article explains 'shifting left' in data engineering—moving data quality checks, business logic, and transformations earlier in the data lifecycle rather than handling them downstream in BI tools. Simon traces the concept from software testing origins through DevSecOps to modern data practices. He argues that declarative, code-first approaches have made shifting left more practical by enabling logic to move simply from one config file to another. The key benefits include earlier error detection, reduced compute costs through pre-materialization, centralized business logic, and enabling domain experts to contribute to data quality upstream.
key_points: Shifting left moves data quality checks and business logic closer to the source/ingestion phase rather than handling them downstream in BI tools or at consumption
Declarative code-first approaches enable shifting logic by simply moving YAML configuration between tools, which was impossible with GUI-first approaches
The local development loop with tools like SQLMesh, DuckDB, and SQL transpilers allows catching bugs during development before they reach production
ELT (extract-load-transform) can be seen as an anti-pattern of shifting left since it moves validations and transformations to the right
Shifting left is also an organizational change—defining that models are centrally managed and business analysts reuse existing aggregations rather than duplicating logic
money_quotes: The strategic decision to move data quality checks, business logic, transformations, and governance processes earlier in the data lifecycle—closer to the source or ingestion phase—rather than handling these concerns downstream in BI tools or at the point of consumption.

We don't need to invest massive effort in exporting large SQL or code from our BI tool, or worse, hugely generated code, and trying to convert that to our low-code ETL anymore. We can move descriptive business logic simply from one config file to another.
Errors identified and fixed earlier (more to the left) are less expensive and typically easier to debug. This was not possible with GUI-first approaches; code-first tools open up new possibilities.
Most data quality problems are code quality problems.
Data quality isn't a data team's problem; it's clearly defined best practices that everyone in the organization understands and can follow.
If 100% of issues were caught during development, data quality would never be a concern.
We save compute by moving code from the BI dashboard, which is executed every time a user clicks on the dashboard, to a pipeline where the query is pre-materialized and only paid for once.
posts.key_insight: Declarative code-first approaches have fundamentally changed what's possible with shifting left—enabling data teams to move business logic upstream simply by copying configuration between tools, catching errors during development rather than in production, and making data quality a collective organizational responsibility rather than an afterthought.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that universal data orchestrators are the essential 'heartbeat' of modern data engineering, addressing challenges like complexity management, heterogeneous architectures, data quality, and governance. He traces the evolution from cron jobs to declarative YAML-based orchestrators, emphasizing the convergence of infrastructure-as-code principles with data pipeline management. The post advocates for centralized orchestration over implicit event-driven approaches for complex data workloads, highlighting Kestra's dual interface paradigm (UI + Code) as a bridge between technical and business users. Späti concludes that modern orchestrators must support software engineering best practices while remaining approachable to non-programmers.
key_points: Data orchestrators solve four core challenges: complexity management, handling heterogeneous architectures, continuous data quality/error handling, and data governance
The evolution from cron to modern orchestrators reflects a shift toward declarative, version-controlled pipelines that support software engineering best practices
Centralized orchestration is preferable over implicit event-driven approaches for complex data workloads because they require end-to-end visibility and governance
The dual interface paradigm (UI + Code) bridges the gap between technical users who need version control and business users who need approachability
YAML has become the lingua franca of modern system orchestration, enabling declarative configuration across DevOps and data engineering
money_quotes: The orchestrator is the heart of any data engineering project, potentially any data platform.

Data workloads are different. These are less time-critical and usually vastly complex with upstream data out of our control; we need to implement hack upon hack, and need to deliver high-quality data to the users.
Without a data orchestrator, you won't have a scheduler, data lineage, visualizer, or handy function to restart a process. You end up with disjointed stored procedures.

Coding is hard, especially for business specialists who need to keep up within their field and domain and just need to get their jobs scheduled. You can't expect them to learn programming on the side.

Generally, software best practices are hard to apply to data engineering. We know that from the history of SSIS and Informatica, GUI-driven tools where you need to click through 100 tables if you want to load tables.

Imagine an airplane; it's vastly complex. No person, not even a pilot, can know the inside out of every jet, every piece. That's why they created a cockpit, with hundreds of lights and knobs to turn and see what's going on.
posts.key_insight: Modern data orchestrators must combine declarative code-first approaches with intuitive UIs to bridge the gap between software engineering best practices and accessibility for business users.

posts.summary: Simon argues that logs are the lifeblood of data platforms and that data engineers spend significant time parsing and analyzing them for debugging, tracing, and performance analysis. Unlike software engineers who can use debuggers, data engineers must 'log their way through problems' because data is constantly changing and flowing from external sources. The post provides a comprehensive tutorial on using DuckDB as a log parser, demonstrating its ability to handle various formats (JSON, CSV, syslog) with zero-copy reading from S3, data warehouses, or HTTPS. For larger datasets that exceed local resources, MotherDuck extends DuckDB's capabilities to cloud scale without syntax changes, enabling collaborative analysis of massive log datasets.
key_points: Data stacks are fundamentally built on logs - they're the primary debugging mechanism since data pipelines can't be debugged like traditional software
DuckDB excels as a log parser due to its fast JSON/CSV parsers, zero-copy reading from multiple sources, and consistent SQL syntax across file formats
LLMs can help generate complex RegExp patterns to parse semi-structured log files by providing the schema description and sample lines
Pre-materializing and unnesting JSON data dramatically improves query performance for analytical workloads
MotherDuck provides seamless scale-up when local DuckDB resources are insufficient, using identical syntax
money_quotes: Logs are the lifeblood of our data platforms
Data platforms are essentially built around logs. We can't debug the data stack; the logs are our way to find the error later on.
Software engineers can debug more easily, as they are in control of what the user can and can't do. But data is different, constantly changing and flowing from A to B.
As we can't simply use a debugger with complex data pipelines, we must log our way through problems.

DuckDB is the ultimate log parser. It can run with zero-copy, meaning you don't need to install or insert logs into DuckDB, but you can read from your data lake in S3, from your Snowflake Warehouse, and from your servers via HTTPS server, all within a single binary.
The better the logs, the less we need to search, and the more we can focus our time on fixing the bugs.
Knowing how to analyze logs straightforwardly and efficiently is a competitive advantage in today's data-driven world.
posts.key_insight: Data engineering platforms are fundamentally built on logs rather than debuggers, making efficient log parsing with tools like DuckDB a critical competitive advantage for data teams.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that declarative data stacks represent the next evolution in enterprise data engineering, combining open-source flexibility with integrated platform capabilities. He traces the journey from monolithic vendor solutions to fragmented modern data stacks, positioning declarative approaches as the synthesis that offers both freedom and cohesion. The core thesis is that YAML-based configuration can replace most custom coding, enabling teams to deliver production-ready platforms faster while maintaining governance. Using Starlake as his primary example, he demonstrates how declarative stacks cover the entire data lifecycle—extract, load, transform, orchestrate—through configuration rather than code. The approach promises reduced technical debt, better reproducibility, and democratized data engineering accessible to non-programmers.
key_points: Declarative data stacks synthesize the best of monolithic platforms and modular open-source tools, offering integration without vendor lock-in
YAML configuration replaces imperative coding, making data pipelines reproducible, version-controlled, and accessible to 'YAML engineers'
Unlike black-box orchestrators like Airflow, declarative stacks are 'data-aware' with column-level lineage and explicit dependencies
The approach scales with complexity—ROI increases as enterprise data landscapes grow more intricate
Local development with DuckDB while targeting production warehouses reduces costs and accelerates iteration cycles
money_quotes: Imagine building enterprise data infrastructure where you write 90% less code but deliver twice the value.
A declarative data stack is a set of tools and, precisely, its configs can be thought of as a single function such as run_stack(serve(transform(ingest))) that can recreate the entire data stack.
Unlike Airflow, where each task has no idea what is happening inside of it, it's a black box.
The return on investment in the platform grows with rising complexity.
Breaking free from black boxes doesn't mean abandoning the open data stack, but rather integrating best-in-class tools under a unified, configuration-driven approach.
The declarative approach offers a sustainable path forward that balances enterprise requirements with development agility.
posts.key_insight: Declarative data stacks represent a paradigm shift where configuration replaces coding, enabling enterprises to manage growing data complexity while reducing technical debt and democratizing data engineering beyond traditional programmers.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that open table formats, particularly Apache Iceberg, are revolutionizing data architecture by enabling database-like features on top of distributed files in object storage. He introduces the 'ICE Stack' (Interoperable, Composable, Efficient) as a framework for understanding managed Iceberg tables, which combine cheap storage with flexible compute options. The article examines how hyperscalers like AWS, Databricks, and Cloudflare are betting heavily on managed Iceberg solutions, evidenced by Databricks' $2B acquisition of Tabular. While celebrating the shift toward open standards and vendor lock-in elimination, Späti also critically examines concerns about proprietary APIs in implementations like AWS S3 Tables, noting the tension between managed convenience and true openness.
key_points: The ICE Stack (Interoperable, Composable, Efficient) represents a shift from proprietary data warehouses to open, standardized data platforms built on table formats like Iceberg
Apache Iceberg is winning the table format war, with Delta Lake and Hudi converging toward it as Databricks invests in Iceberg compatibility
Managed Iceberg services from hyperscalers offer convenience but raise concerns about proprietary APIs that could undermine the openness promise
Zero egress fees from providers like Cloudflare R2 are disrupting traditional cloud economics and enabling new data sharing patterns
The real value in open table formats is eliminating vendor lock-in for compute, allowing organizations to swap query engines without moving data
money_quotes: Data lake table formats essentially allow you to store all your data cost-effectively while reaping the benefits of a warehouse or database.
An ICE Stack shifts from where the data is stored (database/cloud data warehouse, Oracle, Teradata) to a data stack and data platform.
With the vendor data lock-in removed, any vendor offering data computation has the advantage that fewer data platforms get locked into being strictly a 'Snowflake, Databricks, BigQuery, or AWS shop'.
As the data itself doesn't move because it stays in open table formats, we can now easily choose and pick the best engine for every job.
While the managed Iceberg with all the additional services is most welcome, the proprietary API is not.

The true power of this open approach lies in its flexibility—data remains in open formats, engines become interchangeable, and organizations maintain ownership of their most valuable asset: their data.
This is a significant shift from earlier eras of enterprise data infrastructure, where customers chose and were locked into major vendors like SAP, Oracle, or even IBM Mainframes.
posts.key_insight: Open table formats like Iceberg are shifting the data industry from vendor lock-in to a composable architecture where storage becomes commoditized and compute becomes interchangeable, fundamentally changing how organizations own and access their data.

posts.summary: Simon advocates for tech independence through self-hosting and owning your digital infrastructure, arguing that buying your own domain and hosting your own services compounds over time. He shares his personal journey from vim/Neovim to building a homelab, emphasizing that while self-hosting requires effort, the joy comes from learning, using what you build, and not depending on companies that can kill products or raise prices. The post celebrates open-source software and the community that shares code freely, highlighting tools like Listmonk, GoatCounter, and Quartz that power his online presence. His core message: start with a domain, learn Linux fundamentals, and gradually build independence from big tech platforms.
key_points: Owning your domain and self-hosting compounds over time - watching people migrate from WordPress to Medium to Substack losing backlinks and history is painful
Tech independence means not depending on any particular company or software, learned from Derek Sivers
The initial learning curve for self-hosting seems hard but becomes obvious once you overcome the first hurdles
Open-source creates a beautiful community where millions give away work for free, enabling others to build on top
Self-hosting provides independence from companies like Google that routinely kill beloved products or raise prices
money_quotes: If people ask me how they should start writing or how to get a job, I always say to buy a domain first.
Every time they had a new domain. To me, that's so sad.
Initially everything seems hard, but once you know how, it's kind of obvious and less hard.
Tech Independence basically means that you do not depend on any particular company or software.
We always believe we just have to use what's out there to buy, but there are other ways.
Open-source and open-source code is beautiful. It's much more than just using someone else's software, but it's all the millions of people who just give away their work for free.
Linux runs the world. There is almost no digital device that we use that is not running Linux or part of it.
You also don't get a heart attack if Google turns off your favorite app.
posts.key_insight: Self-hosting and tech independence isn't just about avoiding subscription costs—it's about the compounding value of ownership, the joy of building and learning, and freedom from platform dependency.

posts.summary: Simon Späti explores the open lakehouse architecture built on affordable object storage combined with open table formats like Iceberg, Delta Lake, and Hudi. He argues that these formats bring database-like features (ACID transactions, versioning, SQL interfaces) to distributed files without expensive SSD storage. The article demonstrates practical examples using DuckDB and MotherDuck to query Iceberg tables directly from S3, and introduces DuckDB as a lightweight catalog layer using views. Späti positions this as the next evolution of the Databricks Lakehouse concept, emphasizing modularity and vendor independence through interchangeable compute engines and open catalogs like Polaris and Unity Catalog. He concludes that while open data platforms trade some performance for flexibility, they enable truly composable data ecosystems without lock-in.
key_points: Open table formats bundle distributed files into manageable tables with database-like features (ACID, versioning, time travel) on cheap object storage
The open data platform architecture has four interchangeable layers: storage, file format, table format, and catalog - with compute engines as the connecting force
DuckDB can serve as a lightweight catalog by creating small databases with views pointing to remote data, enabling SQL access without complex infrastructure
The battle for data platform control has shifted from compute engines and table formats to the catalog layer, where vendor lock-in remains a challenge
AI-powered workflows using MCP enable natural language querying of Iceberg tables, though human interpretation remains essential for understanding results
money_quotes: Open Table Format bundles distributed files into manageable tables with database-like features. Newer features enhance and facilitate access and data governance, similar to a lakehouse.
The compute layer is responsible for creating files on S3, creating an Iceberg table, or managing the request for the number of tables sent to the catalog via API.
Open Standards over Silos - instead of loading data into proprietary formats of cloud vendors and getting locked in, we work directly with open standards.
This tremendously simplifies the work we have to do on the data engineering side; we can avoid creating denormalization pipelines and data duplication solely for reporting purposes.
No matter how good GenAI or GenBI is, humans are still irreplaceable in interpreting the results and understanding the domain.
It also shows that the English language is not always precise enough. That's why SQL is sometimes better to use or to explain to an LLM, so we communicate exactly what we want.
We might even see a decline in dashboard use per se, as self-service analytics is now possible for the first time via chat with the analytics backend.
posts.key_insight: Open table formats and lightweight compute engines like DuckDB are democratizing the lakehouse architecture, enabling database-like capabilities on cheap storage while avoiding vendor lock-in through interchangeable, standards-based components.

posts.summary: This practical guide explores enterprise-level data orchestration using Kestra, dlt, and Snowflake. The post demonstrates how to build production-ready data pipelines with proper separation of concerns, secret management, and multi-tenancy. Späti emphasizes the 'universal orchestrator' concept where visual UI interfaces generate version-controlled YAML code, making pipelines accessible to both engineers and analysts. The article also covers AI integration through MCP (Model Context Protocol), arguing that YAML-based orchestration is uniquely suited for LLM collaboration because the code artifacts serve as shared context between humans and AI models.
key_points: Universal orchestrators bridge technical complexity and practical usability by combining visual interfaces with generated YAML and Git synchronization
Business logic should be separated from orchestration code - use subflows for reusable components and plugins to abstract technical complexity
Enterprise deployments require proper secret management (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) rather than hardcoded credentials
YAML-based pipelines are ideal for AI collaboration because they provide an 'in-memory RAM' intermediate stage where both LLMs and humans can build larger context together
MCP integration allows AI agents to work autonomously on pipeline creation and maintenance within IDEs like Cursor or Neovim
money_quotes: Moving from orchestration theory to the enterprise level is a real challenge. How do you handle secrets across environments? Where does your business logic actually live?

In the old days, we put it into stored procedures—custom code that databases had like PL/SQL in Oracle and T-SQL in Microsoft SQL Server. In the world of composable data stacks, the business logic is usually in SQL statements, Python, or YAML.
Think of it as a resource that data engineers build, which less technical people can reuse.

Why are code artifacts such as YAML the perfect setup for AI? Because AI has a very limited context window, but if you give it an intermediate stage, think of it like in-memory RAM that both the LLM and the human can work together on.

Data orchestration is becoming more accessible, but the underlying complexity hasn't disappeared. Tools like Kestra handle that complexity so teams can focus on what their data should accomplish rather than how to move it around.
Unlike many other orchestrators, you do not need to know how to code or have a technical background.
posts.key_insight: YAML-based orchestration creates an ideal collaboration layer between humans and AI models, serving as shared context that enables both parties to iteratively build and maintain complex data pipelines.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that conversational BI powered by Model Context Protocol (MCP) finally delivers on the two-decade-old promise of self-serve business intelligence. Unlike traditional BI where self-serve meant filtering dashboards, MCP enables LLMs to autonomously query databases, verify facts, and generate BI artifacts through natural language prompts. The post identifies three distinct output types—numerical data retrieval, AI-generated text interpretations, and visual charts—that combine to create a more powerful workflow than either humans or AI alone. Späti emphasizes that narrowing the analytical context makes AI more deterministic and less prone to hallucination, while semantic layers provide the crucial business context LLMs need to understand domain-specific metrics.
key_points: MCP standardizes AI integration with data tools the way LSP standardized language servers for IDEs, enabling plug-and-play connectivity between any AI model and BI systems
Conversational BI produces three distinct output types: numerical data (factual, non-hallucinated), text interpretations (AI-powered insights), and visuals (for human verification and exploration)
Narrowing the analytical context—focusing on specific data points rather than broad dashboards—makes AI responses more deterministic and accurate
The combination of BI-as-Code, semantic metrics layers, and real-time databases creates the foundation for effective human-AI collaboration
Domain experts remain essential as human oversight ensures AI-generated insights align with business reality and catch potential errors
money_quotes: With the right setup, conversations can generate BI artifacts.
Domain experts know the business and numbers best, so they are also the best ones to check the numbers and iteratively create BI artifacts.
Many data warehouses or data lakes aren't fast enough to support conversation-speed interrogations.
By going narrower, like analyzing a spike in a dashboard, it's much less likely to hallucinate and makes its prediction more deterministic.
Conversational BI is self-serve, too, but it is slower and more imprecise in a grand context, though super helpful in a narrow context.
The key insight from our exploration of conversational BI is fundamental: conversations can generate code, and code generates insights.
Conversational BI finally delivers on the promise of self-serve BI that the industry has pursued for two decades.
posts.key_insight: True self-serve BI becomes possible when LLMs can autonomously query semantic layers through MCP, but effectiveness depends on narrowing analytical context and maintaining human oversight from domain experts.

posts.summary: This second part of Simon Späti's data engineering toolkit series covers advanced infrastructure concerns: data processing tools, DevOps practices, and the expanding role of infrastructure as code. The post argues that modern data engineers must master not just SQL and Python, but also Kubernetes, Terraform, and GitOps practices to build production-grade platforms. Späti presents a comprehensive catalog of tools across databases, orchestration, BI, containerization, and observability, while noting that AI-enhanced workflows are reshaping pipeline development. The piece concludes that soft skills and business understanding are increasingly crucial as AI reduces pure coding demands.
key_points: Data engineers now face infrastructure complexity similar to how data scientists once spent 80% of time on data wrangling—DevOps and IaC have become core competencies
GitOps and declarative configurations transform scattered server management into maintainable, reproducible deployments through YAML-based version-controlled infrastructure
DevOps operates on a spectrum of abstraction levels—from serverless to managed Kubernetes—and teams should match abstraction to expertise rather than defaulting to maximum control
AI-enhanced tools with MCP integrations and agentic workflows are actively reshaping how data engineers build and interact with pipelines
As AI reduces coding demands, business acumen and communication skills become the differentiator that transforms data engineers into 10x contributors
money_quotes: One day, you're optimizing SQL queries, the next, you're debugging Kubernetes deployments, and by afternoon, you'll be explaining data quality metrics to stakeholders who just want to know why their dashboard is empty.
SQL is the language of data. SQL is a fundamental skill for doing any data work. There's almost nothing you do without needing SQL.
DevOps isn't binary; it's about selecting the appropriate level of control and abstraction for your specific needs.
Start with higher abstraction levels for faster delivery, then move toward more control only when specific customizations become necessary.
Setting up GitOps for new data projects is hard and best done by having a central team specializing in deployment and operations with deep knowledge.
While you can be a technical expert without these skills, combining technical expertise with strong business understanding and communication will set you apart.
Technical expertise alone isn't always enough; strong communication skills and business understanding transform data engineers into 10x contributors.
posts.key_insight: Modern data engineering requires mastering a spectrum of DevOps abstraction levels and infrastructure tooling, but as AI handles more coding tasks, soft skills and business understanding become the true differentiators.

posts.summary: Simon Späti explores life's fundamental purpose through a deeply personal two-year journey from secular Stoicism toward Christianity.
