He argues that modern society's abandonment of religion correlates with rising anxiety and depression, while material pursuits and career worship provide only shallow, temporary happiness. Drawing from his wife's faith, diverse philosophical traditions, and biblical study, he concludes that deep relationships—with others and potentially with God—provide life's true meaning. The essay positions Christianity's community, worship, and moral framework as superior to secular alternatives for navigating hardship and finding lasting fulfillment.
key_points: Worshipping material things (career, money, status) leads to shallow happiness that never satisfies, while spiritual worship expects nothing in return and provides deeper fulfillment
The decline of religious belief in modern society correlates with all-time highs in anxiety, depression, and suicide rates, particularly among Gen-Z
Many philosophical frameworks (Stoicism, Tao Te Ching) contain wisdom that traces back to or parallels biblical teachings, but lack the Bible's depth
Churches provide free community, therapy-like support, and the unique power of collective worship that secular institutions cannot replicate
Faith provides a 'daylight' or life compass that helps navigate hardship, which is why people often turn to God during crises rather than prosperity
money_quotes: The best things in life are free, the second best things are very expensive.
We think we are in control.
Churches are a better therapy.
If belief or doubt makes you do something good, then choose that view because it's useful to you.
Work will always be work.
Your life on Earth is a fleeting moment compared to eternity.
Following the secular path, thinking I am in control of everything.
The Bible is the trunk that branches and leaves hang onto.
It's not what you say or want, it's about what you act on.
posts.key_insight: Material and career pursuits provide only shallow happiness; lasting fulfillment comes from deep relationships and surrendering the illusion of control to something greater than oneself.

posts.summary: Simon Späti presents a comprehensive three-week learning roadmap for aspiring data engineers to master essential skills during summer. Week 1 covers foundations (SQL, Git, Linux), Week 2 focuses on core engineering (Python, cloud platforms, data modeling), and Week 3 addresses advanced topics (streaming, data quality, DevOps). The guide emphasizes learning at your own pace with curated resources, stressing that consistency matters more than speed. Späti argues that while you won't know everything after three weeks, you'll understand the problems and identify potential solutions.
key_points: SQL remains the most critical skill for data engineers at any level because it allows precise data specification that natural language through LLMs cannot match
Python serves as the 'glue code that connects everything' you can't achieve with SQL, particularly for external systems and workflow orchestration
Data modeling is 'somewhat underappreciated these days' but has become even more critical with the rise of AI and automation
Consistency in learning (30 minutes to 1-2 hours daily) compounds over time and matters more than intensive cramming
Building an online portfolio and sharing learnings publicly helps land dream roles and creates community connections
money_quotes: Sometimes we forget how far 30 minutes a day can take us.
You can descriptively explain what you want from your data much more precisely than natural language through LLM workflows. That's why it will always be a core skill.
Python is the glue code that connects everything you can't achieve with SQL.
Data modeling is a significant one, and somewhat underappreciated these days. However, with the rise of AI and automation, it hasn't been more critical to learn.
Most often, it's quick to set up a data platform, or a stack to extract analytics from your data, but doing it stably and with high data quality is an entirely different job.
Spending time on the right tasks can save a lot of money.
If you give yourself time to digest, you learn more easily, you'll be able to recall specific terms better, and it's easier to connect the knowledge—this is how our brains learn.
posts.key_insight: Mastering data engineering requires consistent, structured learning across SQL, Python, cloud platforms, and DevOps—with SQL remaining the irreplaceable foundation because it enables precision that natural language cannot match.

posts.summary: Simon Späti documents his transition from a 5-year-old MacBook Pro M1 Max to a Lenovo ThinkBook running Arch Linux with DHH's Omarchy distribution. After 15 years on macOS, he finds the switch surprisingly smooth thanks to Omarchy's opinionated setup that mirrors keyboard-driven workflows. While acknowledging trade-offs like worse battery life, fan noise, and missing tools like Snagit, he emphasizes the joy of customization and learning. The post details his pre-switch requirements, app replacements found for most needs, and hardware choice rationale, ultimately concluding that the freedom to modify his OS outweighs the polish of macOS.
key_points: Omarchy makes Arch Linux approachable for macOS power users by providing sensible defaults for screens, media keys, hibernation, and keyboard-driven navigation out of the box
Most macOS apps have Linux alternatives (Raycast→Walker, Karabiner→Kanata, Sync→Filen), though screenshot tools like Snagit remain unmatched
The trade-offs are real: worse battery life, constant fan noise, occasional breakage requiring manual fixes, and no native apps for tools like Claude Desktop or Microsoft Teams
The customization freedom is the killer feature - being able to script shortcuts for anything, change themes system-wide, and modify the OS to personal preferences
MacBook hardware quality (5+ year lifespan, no fans, great battery) remains superior, but the frustration with Apple's walled garden and breaking tools like Yabai motivated the switch
money_quotes: If I don't like something on my OS, I just change it. Or if I want to improve a certain way of working, a quick script, a new package, and I'm just flying.
I never thought that an OS can be customizable to my liking.
The sky is the limit, literally.
It's so fun to set up your OS in a way that makes you more productive, or gives you joy just by the looks of it.
But stuff like this makes me scared that I can run a wrong command and ruin my perfect setup. I need a backup!
The quality of MacBooks is just another level. I had 3 or 4 so far since 2010, and each of them held at least 5 years. Crazy good.
Before, Windows was unusable and with Arch in the back, it runs almost like a brand-new laptop.
posts.key_insight: The freedom to fully customize your operating system and workflow can outweigh the polish and convenience of macOS, especially for developers frustrated by Apple's increasingly restrictive approach.

posts.summary: The post argues that semantic layers provide crucial value by defining business metrics once in a single source of truth, eliminating duplication across BI tools, web apps, and notebooks. Simon demonstrates this practically by building a minimal semantic layer using YAML definitions and Python/Ibis with DuckDB as the query engine, analyzing 20 million NYC taxi records. He emphasizes that semantic layers are most valuable when you have multiple analytics consumers, complex business logic, or need ad-hoc queries with dynamic granularity. The article distinguishes between physical datasets and logical aggregations, showing how the abstraction layer enables non-technical users to modify metrics without touching code.
key_points: Semantic layers solve metric duplication across multiple BI tools by providing a single source of truth for business logic
The key distinction is datasets vs. aggregations: if you need ad-hoc queries that change granularity on the fly, you need a semantic layer
YAML-based metric definitions combined with query translation layers like Ibis enable non-technical stakeholders to modify business logic without code changes
Semantic layers are overkill when you have only one analytics consumer, simple aggregations, or can preprocess all metrics in SQL transformations
Modern semantic layers can integrate with LLMs/RAG systems to provide business context and improve natural language querying accuracy
money_quotes: With a semantic layer, your revenue KPI or other complex company measures are defined once in a single source of truthâno need to re-implement them over and over again.
The magic is that we can now change the metric in the YAML file, add a CASE WHEN statement, or fix a formatting error all without touching the query or code.

Try implementing a 'revenue per customer by quarter with year-over-year comparison' across five different BI tools using just database viewsâyou'll most probably end up with five different implementations that drift apart over time.
dataset â  aggregations, table columns â  metrics, physical table â  logical definition
The beauty of semantic layers lies in their ability to simply define dependencies on complex measures, eliminating the need to repeat 100 lines of SQL code in your CTE query.
Unlike pre-computed aggregation tables that need to be reprocessed when source data changes, semantic layers generate queries on demand.
posts.key_insight: Semantic layers matter most when you need to maintain consistent business metrics across multiple analytics tools, enabling ad-hoc queries with dynamic granularity while keeping metric definitions in a single, version-controlled location.

posts.summary: This comprehensive guide explains how to model data effectively in ClickHouse for real-time analytics, moving from theory to a practical implementation using NOAA weather data. The author argues that ClickHouse can serve as both storage and transformation engine, eliminating traditional ETL bottlenecks. Key strategies covered include denormalization, materialized views, partitioning, and pre-aggregation using MergeTree engines. The post demonstrates a complete data pipeline from S3 ingestion through ClickHouse transformation to Rill visualization, all configured in a single YAML file. The fundamental principle is minimizing joins at query time while choosing where in the pipeline to handle complexity based on performance and freshness requirements.
key_points: ClickHouse achieves sub-second queries on billions of rows through column-oriented storage, sparse indexing, and vectorized execution - performance that would take minutes in traditional warehouses
Real-time analytics involves a fundamental tradeoff between data freshness and accuracy; the moment data is loaded it's outdated, but consistency across tables must be maintained
MergeTree engines transform ClickHouse into 'local ETL without needing an ETL tool' - data gets ingested, transformed, and optimized automatically
Unlike traditional OLAP cubes that pre-process measures, ClickHouse allows query-time flexibility without reprocessing, though it lacks a logical modeling layer
Data sketches and sampling strategies can reduce storage by 90-99% while maintaining 99%+ accuracy, dramatically cutting costs and improving query speed
money_quotes: Querying billions of weather records and getting results in under 200 milliseconds isn't theory; it's what real-time analytics solutions provide.
If we have the best tools but a bad data flow, it's not worth much.
The moment the data is loaded, it is outdated.
ClickHouse doesn't only serve as storage but can handle much of the transformation and aggregation work directly, eliminating traditional ETL bottlenecks.
The fundamental principle underlying all these approaches is minimizing joins at query time.
MergeTree engines are specifically designed for high data ingest rates and massive data volumes.
Data sketches use clever algorithms to deliver 99%+ accuracy for 1% of the cost and storage.
ClickHouse lets us handle powerful use cases without the need for expensive ETL pipelines or an additional semantic layer.
posts.key_insight: ClickHouse can serve as both your transformation engine and storage layer, handling complex aggregations at ingestion time rather than requiring traditional ETL pipelines, while its modeling strategies let you explicitly choose where in the pipeline to handle complexity based on your specific performance and freshness requirements.

posts.summary: Simon Späti explains why he deliberately avoids traditional research when writing, preferring to draw entirely from personal experience and accumulated knowledge. He views his ongoing note-taking in his 'Second Brain' as a form of passive research that connects ideas over time. With two decades of field experience, he argues that things don't change as drastically as media suggests. His goal as a writer is not to recall information but to spark thinking and inspire readers through authentic, experience-based perspectives.
key_points: Traditional research disrupts the creative writing process and dilutes authentic voice
A Second Brain note-taking system serves as passive, always-on research that connects ideas organically over time
Two decades of hands-on field experience provides more reliable insight than desk research
The goal of technical writing should be to spark ideas and inspire, not merely recall and relay information
Getting hands-on with tools provides a 'feel' that reading about them cannot replicate
money_quotes: The fun of writing is to express myself and explain something in my head. Not researching something and losing focus.
I want to share my opinions.
Things do not drastically change in an instant, even if the media and social media make us believe so.
I see my goal as a writer not to recall info, but to make you think, spark new ideas, inspire at best.
I try the tool and get hands-on. So rather than browsing and reading, I get a feel for it.
I can describe a feeling or experience much better than researching something, and recall them as if they were my words.
posts.key_insight: The most authentic and inspiring technical writing comes from lived experience rather than desk research, because a writer's goal should be to spark new thinking, not to relay information.

posts.summary: This sponsored article explores how declarative data stacks, specifically Starlake, can replace custom ETL code with YAML configurations. Späti argues that most data teams waste 80% of their time on infrastructure plumbing—writing UPSERT logic, building incremental loads, and debugging orchestration failures. He walks through Starlake's capabilities across the full ETL lifecycle: extraction from diverse sources, loading with built-in write strategies like SCD2 and UPSERT, SQL transformation with cross-engine transpilation, automated DAG generation, and testing locally with DuckDB. The core pitch is that a configuration-first approach eliminates glue code and vendor lock-in while delivering enterprise features that normally require dedicated platform teams.
key_points: Declarative data stacks replace custom ETL code with YAML configurations, offering built-in write strategies (SCD2, UPSERT, APPEND) that typically require extensive custom development
SQL transpilation across engines (BigQuery, Snowflake, Databricks, DuckDB) enables local testing with DuckDB before deploying to production warehouses, reducing development cycles and cost
Automatic DAG generation and lineage tracking inferred directly from SQL statements eliminates manual dependency declarations and Jinja references
Multi-engine and cross-engine support avoids vendor lock-in—you can use different compute engines per environment or even per model
The article is a sponsored piece written as part of Späti's consulting services for Starlake
money_quotes: Most data teams spend 80% of their time wrestling with infrastructure—writing custom UPSERT logic, building incremental loading from scratch, or debugging why their Airflow DAGs failed again at 2 AM.
Though the code and license to use the tools cost nothing, it's expensive to set up yourself if you don't have the expertise.
Having these integrated and switching quickly by just changing the writing strategy is handy and significant.
This allows us to test the SQL on top of an in-memory DuckDB database without hitting the data warehouse, inducing more interactivity and less cost.

Testing is most important in data engineering and data analytics platforms because if we have false key performance indicators or get inconsistent data, business consumers of the data won't trust the data anymore.
I believe the future is declarative data engineering, and Starlake can play a big part in this.
posts.key_insight: Declarative YAML-based configurations can replace most custom ETL code, offering enterprise features like SCD2, cross-engine SQL transpilation, and automatic DAG generation without vendor lock-in.

posts.summary: Späti argues that AI agents in analytics need three pillars to work effectively: semantics (structured metric definitions), speed (sub-second query responses), and stewardship (guardrails and human oversight). He proposes Metrics SQL as a higher-level SQL dialect that operates on business concepts rather than raw columns, making it ideal for both human and AI consumption. The article demonstrates how tools like Rill with MCP connections enable agentic workflows where AI can query and create metrics while respecting defined constraints. His core thesis is that data modeling fundamentals haven't changed, but agents now serve as powerful collaborators — more like advanced linters than replacements for human judgment.
key_points: AI agents need structured semantic layers to avoid hallucinating metrics — SQL is more precise than natural language for expressing business logic
Sub-second query response times are critical because latency kills the human ability to verify and iterate on agent-generated numbers
Stewardship through ai_instructions and guardrails is underdeveloped — 78% of executives claim guardrails exist while 79% don't address them properly
Metrics SQL extends standard SQL to reference predefined dimensions and measures, eliminating redundancy while inheriting security policies automatically
Jevons's Paradox applies to data: more AI capability means more humans needed for verification, not fewer
money_quotes: AI agents can theoretically analyze anything, but without the right foundations, they're as likely to hallucinate a metric as to calculate it correctly.
AI needs structure to understand, humans need speed to verify, and both need boundaries to stay productive.
SQL is the language of data that AI agents understand better than natural language. It's more accurate and more detailed, avoiding subtle misunderstandings.
The longer we wait, the less likely we are to thoroughly battle-test the numbers.
With all the liberty that AI gets — such as access to databases, all KPIs, and sensitive context through prompts — comes more risk.
Instead of needing fewer humans, we need more. That's called Jevons's Paradox.
The future of data modeling isn't humans or agents — it's humans with agents, each doing what they do best.
Think of it as a BI pair programmer or an AI linter for business intelligence.
posts.key_insight: Effective agentic analytics requires structured semantic layers that give AI precise business context, sub-second speed that lets humans verify results, and explicit stewardship guardrails — treating agents as advanced linters rather than autonomous replacements.

posts.summary: This article presents a roundtable discussion where four senior data engineers (Ben Rogojan, Julien Hurault, Mehdi Ouazza, and Simon Späti) answer the top 10 most-upvoted questions from r/dataengineering. The questions span interview preparation, data quality, warehouse vs lakehouse decisions, schema changes, real-time requirements, inheriting legacy systems, Linux skills, Excel exports, cloud costs, and career wisdom. The experts consistently emphasize fundamentals over trendy tools, understanding business context, starting simple and iterating, and the importance of communication with stakeholders. The format draws from Reddit's 174K-member community to surface the real challenges data engineers face daily.
key_points: Fundamentals and principles beat chasing the latest tools—focus on core data engineering lifecycle skills for interviews and daily work
Data quality requires business context and experience; use the WAP (write, audit, publish) technique and start with basic tests before expanding
Push back on real-time requirements—90% of the time batch processing serves business needs without the added complexity
Schema changes are 'a people problem, not tooling'—upstream producers rarely own responsibility for downstream analytics impacts
The best architecture isn't the newest one; it's the one that helps your team deliver value faster and more reliably
money_quotes: Fundamentals and principles beat the latest tool or technology.
It's better to have no data than bad data.
The best architecture isn't the newest one; it's the one that helps your team deliver value faster and more reliably.
It's a people problem, not tooling. The reason things break is that upstream producers don't own the responsibility downstream for analytics.
I'm always pushing back the need for 'real-time,' and it has worked 90% of the time.
Data engineers should build pipelines the same way startup founders build products.
The biggest pitfall is trying to recreate everything to your 'taste.' Accept the legacy and adapt or improve one thing at a time.
Don't let other companies' tech diagrams and system designs be the only thing that guides you.
posts.key_insight: Success in data engineering comes from understanding business context and stakeholder needs, not from mastering the trendiest tools—fundamentals, communication, and pragmatic simplicity consistently outweigh technical complexity.

posts.summary: Simon argues that we're at a cultural turning point where people are rejecting algorithm-driven experiences in favor of owning things, using single-purpose devices, and embracing distraction-free tools. He advocates for boredom as a luxury that heals mental health and sparks creativity, contrasting the shallow consumption of algorithmic feeds with the deep, transformative experience of reading books. The post weaves together observations about technology backlash with personal book recommendations and reflections on writing from a blank page. He concludes that the pendulum is swinging back toward intentional, human-centered experiences over corporate-optimized ones.
key_points: People are returning to single-use devices and local-first tools (old iPods, typewriters, DuckDB, Obsidian) to escape algorithmic control
Boredom is a luxury that heals mental state and unlocks creativity - we should embrace not doing anything
Books offer deeper learning than podcasts because they take years to write and have well-structured ideas, plus the Lindy Effect validates older works
We're tired of being 'the product' and want tools that benefit us rather than corporate pockets
Writing from scratch each time, like Murakami suggests, leads to continuous improvement
money_quotes: Being bored is the new luxury.
We, the people who are the product, are tired of the algorithm telling us what we listen to, watch, or write about.
If you wait one minute or two, something will always come to our minds. In my experience, there is no shortage of ideas.
Embrace not doing anything, embrace Boredom, Daydream. Follow your Intuition.
Writing a book takes a year or more. The structure and ideas are well thought out, not just made up on the spot.
Devices that are uni-taskers, doing one thing well.
Boredom isn't a bug, it's a feature.
posts.key_insight: The backlash against algorithmic control is driving people toward single-purpose tools, owned media, and intentional boredom as antidotes to attention exploitation.

posts.summary: Simon Späti presents a comprehensive tutorial for building a multi-cloud cost analytics dashboard that unifies AWS, GCP, and Stripe revenue data into a single view. The project uses dlt for data extraction, DuckDB/Parquet for storage, and Rill for visualization. He documents the unexpected complexity of cloud cost exports—AWS requires S3 bucket setup, GCP needs BigQuery configuration—before data can even be extracted. The end result is a composable, code-first solution that calculates margins by combining cloud costs with revenue, giving businesses visibility they typically lack. He also demonstrates how AI coding agents excel with declarative data stacks where everything is local and code-defined.
key_points: Cloud cost visibility across providers is surprisingly difficult because each vendor (AWS, GCP) requires manual portal configuration before any API access works
The project combines three data sources via dlt pipelines: AWS CUR exports from S3, GCP billing from BigQuery, and Stripe revenue via API
Dimensional modeling unifies disparate cost schemas around common dimensions (region, service, time, provider) and facts (amount, revenue, margin)
The declarative, code-first architecture makes the project ideal for AI agents since data is local Parquet files and all configuration is in YAML/Python
The FOCUS Specification provides a FinOps standard for common taxonomy across cloud billing datasets that could further unify multi-cloud analysis
money_quotes: What's missing is the combination of income and cost with a recurring margin board.

Companies often use multiple platforms simultaneously: Lovable for apps, OpenAI and Claude for AI, AWS and GCP for infrastructure. Tracking costs across these services becomes nearly impossible without a unified dashboard.

After I got started, I quickly noticed that wasn't going to be that easy. You need to set up a Cost export report within the portal of AWS, store the data in an S3 bucket, and then from there you can download the data in the appropriate format.
This project started with what seemed like a simple idea: pull costs from a few APIs, throw them in a dashboard, done. But cloud cost data doesn't work that way.
Everything is made composable, meaning you can add extra extraction with dlt or query and visualize additional data with Rill without spinning up infrastructure.
Python is the language of data and Claude understands it better than I do.
If you want, you can vibe code new data sources into the project.
posts.key_insight: Building multi-cloud cost visibility requires navigating each provider's unique export mechanisms before any real analytics work can begin, but a composable stack of dlt, DuckDB, and Rill makes the unified dashboard achievable and extensible.

posts.summary: The post explores applying Git-like version control workflows to data management, addressing the challenge of testing and rolling back data pipelines in production environments. Simon argues that while Git revolutionized code versioning, data presents unique constraints: tables must remain consistent across relationships, production datasets are massive, and copying data for testing is slow and expensive. The solution lies in leveraging metadata catalogs and zero-copy cloning through technologies like Prolly Trees, which enable instant branching of production data without physical duplication. The article establishes a data movement efficiency spectrum from metadata-based versioning (most efficient) to full data copying (least efficient), setting up a Part 2 that will examine specific tools implementing these concepts.
key_points: Git doesn't work for data because it was designed for small text-based code changes, not large binary files or datasets with schemas and relationships
The efficiency spectrum for data versioning runs from metadata/catalog-based versioning (no data movement) through zero-copy, delta-based approaches, to full 1:1 copying (least efficient)
Branching for data differs from code: you don't merge test branches back to production, you test prod data on dev then roll out code changes
Prolly Trees (Merkle Trees) enable Git-like versioning for databases by splitting data into hash-based chunks where identical content produces identical fingerprints
Software engineers can mock data easily while data engineers have hard dependencies on production data, requiring heavy compute in development rather than production
money_quotes: Remember the 2 AM on-call duty when a recent data pipeline broke the production environment? A data pipeline you've never touched just corrupted customer records.
But what if you could branch your data like you branch code? Test on real data, discard changes instantly, and deploy with confidence.
Data might be stored on a data lake, maybe on a database, or a key-value store like Redis. The data might be huge, containing the full CRM or all sales transactions over the last years.

With code, different features can be developed independently and then merged into the main branch at the end. With data, it's more about testing prod data on dev and then rolling out the code changes to prod.
Instead of moving data, we just create one new or many new pointers. The result is creating new datasets instantly, as it's just a metadata process, and not an actual data transfer.
The key insight is that no matter how you modify data, identical content always produces identical fingerprints.
Git for data is harder than version control for code because we're not just tracking changes but managing state, often at a massive scale.
posts.key_insight: Git-like workflows for data require metadata-based zero-copy cloning rather than physical data duplication, enabling instant branching of production datasets for testing without the time and cost of traditional environment setup.

posts.summary: This tutorial extends a local FinOps project to cloud-native infrastructure using ClickHouse Cloud and Rill Cloud for multi-cloud cost analytics. Building on a previous local Parquet-based solution, Späti demonstrates how dlt's destination flexibility makes switching from local files to ClickHouse trivial with just an environment variable change. The article walks through ingesting AWS CUR, GCP cost, and Stripe data, deploying interactive dashboards to Rill Cloud, and automating the pipeline with GitHub Actions. The hardest challenges weren't the tools themselves but the coordination work: SQL dialect differences between DuckDB and ClickHouse, hard-coded table names, and data anonymization requirements.
key_points: dlt's destination abstraction makes switching from local Parquet to ClickHouse Cloud as simple as changing an environment variable
FinOps is about maximizing business value through cloud spending visibility, not just cost cutting
The declarative data stack approach keeps all configuration in code, making automation and reuse much easier than UI-locked cloud configurations
SQL dialect differences between databases (like date functions) remain a persistent data engineering pain point requiring templating workarounds
AI coding assistants help with glue code but tend to add rather than refactor, requiring careful human oversight to prevent project bloat
money_quotes: The goal of FinOps is to maximize revenue or business value through the cloud. It isn't to save money per se, but it certainly helps with that too.
That's it. That was the whole magic. Thanks to dlt, this is a piece of cake.
The SQL dialects are not aligned, and every database has a little different flavour of date and other functions.

Once we have that figured out, Claude Code and others can help us a ton. But also, typically they make the project much larger. They tend to add code instead of refactoring as they are afraid to break existing code.
In the first version, you are happy it works, then you start optimizing speed, then security and ultimately you share it with others to make it reusable.
That's one advantage that makes this project so powerful. With Rill and dlt, we have everything locally in the repo, in our control to automate and configure from one place.
posts.key_insight: A declarative, code-first data stack with tools like dlt and Rill provides the flexibility to easily switch between local and cloud deployments while keeping full control over configuration and automation.

posts.summary: The post explores how DuckDB and its extensions offer a simpler alternative to traditional OLAP cubes for caching analytical workloads. Simon traces the history of BI caching from data warehouses through OLAP cubes to modern zero-copy approaches, arguing that DuckDB's lightweight binary and transparent caching extensions like QuackStore and cache_httpfs can dramatically speed up dashboards without requiring separate ingestion pipelines. He demonstrates practical examples showing 10-15x query speedups and introduces the concept of 'Frozen DuckLake' for immutable reference data. The core thesis is that the database itself can now serve as the cache, eliminating traditional cache invalidation complexity.
key_points: Caching compensates for imperfect data architectures by providing fast query responses without re-architecting the entire data flow
DuckDB's ecosystem offers transparent read-through caching via extensions (QuackStore, cache_httpfs) that require minimal configuration - just install and set a path
The history of BI caching shows a pendulum swing from server-side to client-side and back, with DuckDB representing a convergence of simplicity and performance
Immutable data patterns like Frozen DuckLake sidestep cache invalidation entirely - the database becomes the cache
Practical benchmarks show query times dropping from ~50 seconds to ~3 seconds with local caching enabled
money_quotes: What we want is simplicity of a database, but the speed of a cache.
You can compensate for an initial bad architecture and still get quick response times and make the frontend more valuable.
OLAP cubes are essentially a cache too. But what we want is a cache that takes us less effort to build.
The cache becomes the database. Or rather, the database becomes the cache.
Sometimes the best cache pattern is no pattern at all, just well-defined principles or a simple extension that can be installed easily.
In times where everyone is vibe coding and not really architecting data applications, at least not in the beginning, the problem of slow result sets and dashboards will appear near instantly.
Caching means constantly duplicating data, storing it optimally, and updating data in case the source changes.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB's lightweight caching extensions offer a pragmatic middle ground between slow uncached queries and the operational complexity of traditional OLAP systems, letting the database itself serve as the cache.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that the data platform landscape is shifting from fragmented 'Modern Data Stack' approaches toward unified, opinionated platforms that handle infrastructure decisions for you. Using the Japanese concept of 'Omakase' (chef's choice) as a metaphor, he contrasts the flexibility of open-source tool assemblies against the operational simplicity of integrated platforms. He emphasizes that open standards (like Parquet, Iceberg, Arrow) are the crucial bridge enabling both approaches to coexist and data to remain portable. The post concludes that DevOps complexity is the biggest challenge in data engineering today, and opinionated platforms solve this by providing battle-tested infrastructure, native observability, and separation of concerns out of the box.
key_points: Opinionated data platforms trade flexibility for immediate time-to-value by making architectural decisions upfront, eliminating weeks of CI/CD, Kubernetes, and integration work
Open standards (Parquet, Iceberg, Arrow) are more important than open-source code because they ensure data portability and prevent vendor lock-in regardless of platform choice
DevOps is the new bottleneck in data engineering—deploying tools reliably at scale is far harder than using them locally for analytics
The Modern Data Stack fragmentation creates hidden costs: debugging across multiple tools, complex upgrades, knowledge silos, and high maintenance burden
Market consolidation (Fivetran+dbt, Databricks, Snowflake) signals a shift toward bundled, end-to-end platforms over best-of-breed tool assembly
money_quotes: Think of it like Omakase, a chef's choice: you leave it up to the data engineers, the ones who built an all-cohesive data platform.
The math is simple. Time spent integrating tools is time not spent delivering value.
DevOps is the new data engineering of data science, just as a decade ago when we did data science, we essentially ended up spending 80% of our time on data engineering.
Building on openness with open standards is a bet on sustainability and gives you trust and visibility in return.
Data is the most valuable part of a company, and if we lose it, most companies lose their edge and, frankly, their reason to be around.
Choose open source when you have technical expertise and need maximum flexibility. Choose opinionated closed source when you need to deliver analytics quickly without the maintenance overhead.

Choosing an open-source tool, using it locally, and doing advanced analytics is the easy part. But deploying it for the entire company at a scale that just works in a reliable way is really difficult.
posts.key_insight: The choice between open-source flexibility and opinionated platforms is increasingly bridged by open standards, but DevOps complexity makes unified platforms the pragmatic choice for teams prioritizing time-to-value over customization.

posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that algorithm-dominated platforms are destroying our well-being by constantly grabbing attention and preventing genuine human connection. He identifies three foundational pillars for a good life: health, family/community, and self-contentment (avoiding greed). The essay critiques how social media addiction leads to shallow happiness and broken relationships, while advocating for a return to the open web through protocols like AT Protocol and Bluesky. Späti sees personal blogs and direct follower connections as the antidote to algorithmic control, predicting that as people tire of AI-generated content and platform dependence, they'll return to authentic human-created content and real connections.
key_points: The three pillars of well-being are health, family/community bonds, and non-greedy self-contentment - money is just a distraction from meaningful life
Algorithm-dominated platforms have replaced the interconnected open web, making us dependent on a handful of dopamine-hitting sites that harm mental health
The 'Death of the Follower' phenomenon means we can't even reach people who chose to follow us - platforms prioritize engagement over connection
AT Protocol and Bluesky represent a return to user-owned data and direct connections, where you can actually reach your followers
Personal blogs are poised for a comeback as people seek authenticity over AI slop and unified social media templates
money_quotes: Algorithms know more about us than our spouses while grabbing our attention constantly.
A healthy, deep, meaningful, happy life might actually look boring from the outside.
We can't even reach our own followers anymore on social media platforms, though they decided to follow us.
Sometimes I'd rather see the prompt, as this is where the genuineness, the soul and curiosity of the person is.
In a way, money is just a distraction from living a meaningful life.
With AI, people feel they don't need to learn programming, writing, or any craft. But I think that's the wrong conclusion.
Big tech does not have the interest of the people. Instead of helping us, which they did in the beginning, they want to profit more off us and make us more addicted.
I believe this will be the future: real connections, real content, real stories.
posts.key_insight: Escaping algorithm-dominated platforms for open protocols and personal blogs isn't just about technology choice - it's essential for reclaiming the foundational pillars of well-being: health, family connection, and freedom from status-seeking greed.

posts.summary: Simon Späti reflects on his 20+ year career in data engineering, tracing the field's evolution from Business Intelligence in the 1980s through Big Data to today's AI-assisted workflows. He argues that despite constant tool changes, data engineers solve the same fundamental problems: ingesting, modeling, transforming, and serving data. The post emphasizes that mastering fundamentals—data modeling, SQL, and understanding business needs—matters far more than chasing new frameworks. Späti positions data engineers as 'invisible plumbers' whose work goes unnoticed until something breaks, and advocates for embracing this foundational role rather than seeking credit.
key_points: The core problems of data engineering—ingestion, modeling, transformation, serving—have remained constant for 50 years despite radical tool changes
Data engineers are 'invisible plumbers' who get noticed only when things break, not when they succeed
Excel files and legacy systems often contain validated business logic that engineers should learn from rather than dismiss
Real-time data requirements are usually overstated; most businesses don't actually need sub-hourly refreshes
Schema changes are primarily people problems (communication, ownership) rather than technology problems
money_quotes: You ingest data. You model it. You transform it. You serve it. Someone asks for a change. Everything breaks. You rebuild. This is the loop.
The tools change. The loop doesn't.
When you do your job, you're invisible. When anything goes wrong, you're under a microscope.
Excel isn't the enemy. Excel is the business telling you what they actually need.
We're not really any smarter than the people before us. We just have better marketing.
Schema changes are usually people problems, not technology problems.
The best data engineers aren't the ones who know every new tool. They're the ones who know why the data matters.
That's the job. Not the tools. Not the frameworks. Not the buzzwords. The moment when data helps a human make a better decision.
posts.key_insight: Despite 50 years of tool evolution from star schemas to AI agents, data engineering fundamentals remain unchanged—and the engineers who master business understanding and data modeling will always be more valuable than those who chase the latest frameworks.

posts.summary: This sponsored deep-dive examines why companies like Coinbase, Pinterest, and Fresha chose StarRocks over alternatives like ClickHouse, Druid, and Pinot for their analytics needs. Through interviews with engineers at these companies, Späti identifies a common pattern: customer-facing analytics on cloud data warehouses like Snowflake became too slow, and teams needed sub-second query responses without heavy pre-denormalization. StarRocks' key differentiator is its ability to perform fast distributed joins natively, enabled by colocated joins, a cost-based optimizer, and multi-tier caching. The article concludes that StarRocks excels when joins are central to your analytics, while ClickHouse remains stronger for single-table observability workloads. Despite the advantages, good data modeling is still essential, and the smaller community and operational complexity are real tradeoffs.
key_points: StarRocks' colocated join mechanism eliminates network shuffle by ensuring tables sharing the same bucket key, count, and replica placement are physically co-located on the same nodes

Multiple companies followed the same migration pattern: Snowflake/Druid got too slow for customer-facing analytics, and StarRocks provided sub-second queries without requiring heavy pre-denormalization in Flink or Spark
The hybrid hot/cold deployment pattern emerged consistently: streaming recent data into StarRocks while federating over Iceberg for cold historical data on S3
Pinterest achieved 50% p90 latency reduction while using only 32% of previous infrastructure after migrating from Druid to StarRocks

Tradeoffs include a smaller community than ClickHouse, operational complexity requiring dedicated engineering resources, and the need to carefully choose partition and distribution keys across colocated tables
money_quotes: You can't overcome the laws of physics, reading from S3 is just slow.

Simply put, StarRocks naturally fits much better for multi-table join scenarios especially for e-commerce and finance sectors. And ClickHouse has better out-of-box templates for observability use cases.
You can only optimize for one of them and leverage the index for the other one. That's a trade-off you have to do.
Choose StarRocks when joins are central to your analytics.
Almost all good designs come down to good data architecture and modeling your data flow.
The key insight is that you don't build all these layers upfront. You start with normalized data, query it directly, and see if it's fast enough.
People are more willing to try and learn the technology that they have heard about or their friends can mention.
Advanced features don't come without tradeoffs. You have to choose a partition key across colocated tables, but you can only optimize for one of them.
posts.key_insight: StarRocks' colocated joins and multi-tier caching let teams skip the expensive pre-denormalization step in Flink/Spark, but good data modeling and careful partition key planning remain essential for realizing those gains.

posts.summary: Simon Späti shares his 8-month experience after switching from macOS to Arch Linux with Omarchy as his daily driver. He details the Linux replacements for his macOS apps including Walker Launcher for Raycast, Morgen for calendar, and Filen for backups, finding many alternatives equal or superior. He documents running Windows inside Linux via Docker for Microsoft Office, and upgrading to a Tuxedo InfinityBook Pro 14 with 128GB RAM. While praising the terminal-native workflow and customizability, he honestly catalogs significant pain points including GPU crashes, hibernation bugs, and WiFi firmware issues. He concludes that Claude Code was essential for troubleshooting and that Linux rewards tinkering with deeper understanding and lasting fixes.
key_points: Walker Launcher proved a comprehensive Raycast replacement covering fuzzy search, clipboard management, emoji search, and file content search on Linux
Running Windows inside Linux via Docker (Dockur) is now easier than running Windows natively, with Simon's PR merged into Omarchy core
Hardware concerns about leaving Apple are overblown — the Tuxedo InfinityBook with 128GB RAM matched MacBook M1 Max battery life and exceeded it in raw specs
Claude Code is described as a 'non-negotiable lifesaver' for Linux troubleshooting, and Simon admits he probably wouldn't have stayed on Linux without it
Linux's advantage isn't that everything works out of the box, but that when something breaks you can actually fix it permanently, unlike proprietary OS updates that silently reset your preferences
money_quotes: It's now easier to run Windows on Linux than natively on a Windows machine.
If you need something, you just build it with Claude Code and integrate it into your laptop. No need to ask Mr. Bill Gates or Tim Cook to integrate it.
Probably, without Claude Code, I wouldn't have made the switch, or I would have made it, but probably wouldn't have stayed.

What I like about Linux: it might not work out of the box for every laptop or every program, but you can actually fix it, and from that moment you know the problem, you learned something about computers, and the error will not appear again.
Unlike other operating systems that change stuff you set in settings for a reason, only to learn that certain updates turned that checkmark back on.

My favorite part aside from customization is just that I don't care about my machine at all: it gets lost? breaks? stolen? I get a new machine, run 1 command and everything is back exactly as I left it.

When something happens, e.g., a crash out of nowhere, I just open Claude and say: I had a crash, I am running Arch Linux, please check the logs what went wrong. And what I get is a full analysis of what went wrong, some fixes and suggestions.
posts.key_insight: Switching from macOS to Linux is now viable for power users thanks to Omarchy's polish and AI-powered troubleshooting with Claude Code, trading Apple's seamless defaults for deeper customizability and permanent fixes.

posts.summary: Simon Späti built a personal knowledge assistant using RAG on his Obsidian vault, with DuckDB as the vector database locally and MotherDuck for a serverless web app via WASM. The system uses BGE-M3 embeddings to enable semantic search across nearly 9,000 markdown notes, leveraging Obsidian's backlink graph to boost results and surface hidden connections between notes. He emphasizes the local-first approach to keep sensitive notes private. The article doubles as a reflection on AI agents in data engineering, arguing that 'plan mode' in tools like Claude Code is the real productivity breakthrough — not smarter models. He cautions that vibe coding and AI agents still require a human architect to avoid generating unmaintainable code that solves the wrong problem.
key_points: DuckDB works surprisingly well as a combined relational and vector database, handling embeddings and cosine similarity in a single file without a dedicated vector DB
The real AI productivity boost comes from plan mode — structured planning before execution — not from models getting smarter
Local-first RAG preserves privacy and gives better results because the context is your own curated notes, not generic internet data
Graph-boosted search that combines semantic similarity with Obsidian's backlink structure surfaces better results than either approach alone
Vibe coding works best when extending an existing framework or skeleton, not starting from scratch — config-driven development with a human architect in the loop
money_quotes: I was surprised how well DuckDB handles this without a dedicated vector database, one file for relational data and vectors together.
The hidden connections this tool surfaces are valuable only because they're my connections, my thinking, not just crawled information on the internet.
Let them run without direction, and you'll get a thousand lines solving the wrong problem.
I get the perception of being super productive, but after a couple of hours, or sometimes days, we actually didn't achieve what we needed.
The character and soul of the person gets stripped away. The quirky things someone does, which make them who they are, that takes away from the fun of writing.
Always keep it simple, because it's easy to make it complex. The true beauty lies in making it simple, which is something agents are not good at.
Human in the seat and config-driven development is what it comes down to with AI agents.
The hard part is coming up with the spec, talking to business, etc. The coding part is the small part.
posts.key_insight: AI agents accelerate building but the real productivity unlock is plan mode — structured human-in-the-loop planning before autonomous execution — not smarter models.
